title: Enterprise OKR Transition — Driving Strategic Alignment
situation: After a period of fragmented goal-setting across regions, the organization committed to transitioning to OKRs to improve strategic alignment, accountability, and execution clarity across a multi-billion-dollar global business. Leaders were working from incompatible priority lists, and there was no shared language for measuring success beyond financials.
obstacle: Most leaders and teams were unfamiliar with OKRs and skeptical they would add value. There was genuine fear that the framework would become bureaucratic overhead — another reporting layer that consumed time without changing behaviour. Some feared losing the autonomy they had to define their own team goals. And critically, OKRs only work if they cascade and connect — convincing every layer of leadership to write genuinely measurable, outcome-driven objectives required sustained coaching and significant rework.
action: Designed the enterprise OKR framework and governance model from scratch, translating strategic priorities into a structure that worked across three regions and multiple business lines. Facilitated workshops with executives and cross-functional teams to draft and stress-test objectives. Built templates, worked examples, and coaching guides so leaders could write OKRs that were specific enough to be useful. Coached leaders individually on the difference between output OKRs ('launch the product') and outcome OKRs ('increase engagement by 15%'). Integrated OKRs into planning, reporting, and performance review cycles so they became part of how the business operated — not a separate exercise.
result: Enterprise-wide OKR adoption achieved across Global W&AM. Leaders who were initially skeptical became advocates once they experienced the clarity it created in prioritization conversations. Alignment between strategy and execution improved measurably, and the shared OKR language became a tool for resolving cross-team conflicts about what mattered most.
impact: Enterprise OKR adoption across Global W&AM; improved strategic alignment across three regions
fullStory: The business had been running on misaligned goal-setting for years — every team had their own priorities, and there was no shared framework for connecting daily work to enterprise strategy. When the decision was made to move to OKRs, I was asked to design and lead the transition. I knew the biggest risk wasn't the framework itself — it was convincing skeptical leaders that this wasn't just another overhead exercise. So I started with the why: I built the case that OKRs were about focus, not bureaucracy, and I showed examples of what good looked like before asking anyone to write their own. The workshops were where the real work happened — I'd push back on outputs masquerading as outcomes, and help leaders get specific about what change they were actually trying to drive. By the time we integrated OKRs into planning and reviews, they had shifted from a compliance exercise into the actual language of strategic prioritization. People started using them to resolve disagreements — 'does this align with our key results or not?' That was the moment I knew it had worked.

title: Advice Strategy — Designing the Future of Member Guidance
situation: Manulife Retirement's advice offerings had grown organically over the years — different products, channels, and service models operating without a coherent strategy. Members received fragmented guidance depending on which touchpoint they used, and the business had no unified view of where advice fit in the member journey or what it was worth financially.
obstacle: Different teams owned different parts of the advice ecosystem — product, digital, contact centre, planning tools — and no single team had accountability for the whole. There was no competitive benchmark to assess where Manulife stood, and no financial model to justify investing in advice as a retention driver. The instinct of most leaders was to optimize within their silo, not redesign the whole.
action: Conducted a comprehensive assessment of the existing advice ecosystem — mapping all member touchpoints, evaluating the quality and consistency of guidance at each stage, and identifying gaps against member needs across life stages. Analyzed competitive offerings and market trends to understand where the industry was heading. Designed an integrated advice strategy with clear value propositions for each member segment, built an investment case showing the retention and engagement value of better advice access, and presented a five-year roadmap for product, digital, and service enhancements to executive leadership.
result: Leadership aligned on a unified advice strategy for the first time — a single articulation of how Manulife would help members make better financial decisions across their entire relationship with the business. A clear roadmap was created for product, digital, and service investment, and advice was repositioned from a nice-to-have to a core retention driver.
impact: Unified five-year advice strategy adopted; advice repositioned as core retention driver
fullStory: When I took on the advice strategy work, the honest reality was that nobody fully owned it. Product owned the tools, digital owned the app experience, the contact centre owned the conversations — but nobody owned the outcome of a member getting good guidance when they needed it. My first job was to build a picture of what was actually happening: where members were looking for advice, what they were getting, and where they were falling through the gaps. The competitive analysis was revealing — we weren't leading, and members who got good guidance stayed with us at dramatically higher rates. That became the financial case. Once I had the data, the strategy practically wrote itself: simplify access, personalize the experience to life stage, and measure retention as the primary KPI for advice investment. The executive alignment was tougher — three different team heads had to see themselves in a strategy they hadn't written. I got there by making sure each one could point to a specific initiative in the roadmap that addressed their team's priorities.

title: KPI Redesign — Eliminating Metrics That Penalized Growth
situation: The business was being measured by KPIs that were actively working against it. The flagship revenue metric was a percentage-of-revenue figure that mathematically punished the business for growing — the bigger the denominator got, the harder it was to hit the target. Leaders were optimizing for the wrong outcomes without realizing it, and the KPI suite as a whole was misaligned with what success actually looked like at the enterprise level.
obstacle: Executives had been using these KPIs for years and had built strategies, incentives, and reporting rhythms around them. Changing them required restating historical performance in a different frame — which meant some periods that had looked strong suddenly looked less impressive. Regional CFOs had vested interests in specific metrics. The political challenge was significant: you're asking leaders to accept that the scorecard they've been measured on was flawed.
action: Systematically analyzed the behavioural impacts of each existing KPI — not just whether it measured the right thing, but what behaviour it was actually incentivizing. Identified the percent-of-revenue metric as the most damaging: it capped ambition on the absolute revenue side by shifting focus to the ratio. Partnered with regional CFOs across Canada, the US, and Asia to redesign the KPI suite around diversified revenue categories, absolute growth, and outcome-based measures. Eliminated the misaligned metric. Built the Participant Outcome Index as a new KPI that measured what the business was actually trying to achieve: retirement readiness. Socialized the changes with before-and-after examples showing how the new framework would have told a different and more accurate story of past performance.
result: Leadership adopted the redesigned KPI framework globally across three regions. Executive attention shifted from managing ratios to driving absolute revenue growth. The Participant Outcome Index was recognized with the Ovation Award and became a cornerstone of the enterprise strategy narrative.
impact: KPI framework reset globally; Participant Outcome Index created and adopted at ELT level; Ovation Award
fullStory: The percent-of-revenue KPI was one of those metrics that looked sensible on the surface but was quietly wrecking the business's ambition. When your profitability denominator grows, your ratio gets worse — which means success punishes you. I started building the case by showing what the numbers would have looked like under the old metric in a strong growth year: the narrative would have been negative despite excellent absolute performance. That was the moment CFOs started listening. The harder conversation was Participant Outcomes — creating a metric that measured retirement readiness required real data work, a defensible methodology, and the courage to measure something that wasn't easy to look good at. We built it anyway, because if we weren't measuring retirement readiness we were building a business without knowing whether we were actually doing our job. The Ovation Award recognition was nice, but the real win was watching the KPIs become the language of strategy conversations rather than a compliance report.
notes: Ovation Award winner

title: Global Metric Alignment — NARM DAWG
situation: Global Retirement teams across Canada, the US, and Asia were using different definitions for the same metrics. 'Net sales' meant something different in Toronto than it did in Boston. 'Capture rate' was calculated three different ways across three regions. Every cross-regional leadership meeting contained a first agenda item that was essentially 'which number is right' — and the answer was always 'it depends on whose definition you're using.'
obstacle: Each region had built their definitions based on their own data systems, business models, and legacy reporting. There was genuine belief in each region that their approach was correct — and they could make a technically defensible case for it. Forcing standardization top-down had been tried before and failed. The CDO team had tried to mandate unified definitions; regional teams had found workarounds. The politics of who 'owns' metrics was real.
action: Established NARM DAWG — the North America Retirement Metrics Definition and Working Group — as a principle-based forum rather than a mandate-driven project. Instead of arriving with a set of definitions to approve, started by getting each region to articulate the business question their metric was designed to answer. When you frame it that way, you quickly find that most of the apparent disagreements are actually about different questions — not different answers. Facilitated working sessions that mapped definitional differences to their root causes, built a unified metric framework that resolved the genuine conflicts, and created governance documentation that gave regional CFOs confidence the standards would be maintained. Socialized the framework with global leadership before formal adoption.
result: Unified global metric definitions formally adopted across all regions and approved by global leadership. Cross-regional reporting became genuinely comparable for the first time. The recurring 'which number is right' conversation largely disappeared from leadership meetings.
impact: Global metric alignment achieved across all regions; eliminated cross-regional reporting confusion
fullStory: The NARM DAWG story is really a story about how to change people's minds when they think they're already right. Every region had a defensible position on their metric definitions. If I'd walked in and said 'you're wrong and here's the right answer,' I'd have gotten nowhere — that's exactly what previous attempts had done. Instead I built the working group around a shared question: what business decision is this metric supposed to inform? Once you ask it that way, most of the disagreement evaporates. Canada and the US weren't measuring different things because they were stubborn — they were measuring different business models. The real work was finding the definition that accurately described both models, which usually meant making the definition more precise than anyone had been comfortable with before. The governance piece was equally important: I made sure each regional CFO could see themselves as co-owners of the framework, not subjects of a mandate. That's what made it stick.

title: Transforming Reporting Culture — From Number-Senders to Strategic Advisors
situation: When I inherited the insights and reporting team, the culture was deeply entrenched in 'number-sending.' Every Monday a report went out. It contained data. It had no narrative, no interpretation, no recommendation. Leaders would receive it, flip through it, and set it aside — or worse, call to ask what the numbers meant and get a defensive response about how the team's job was to provide the data, not interpret it. The insights function was being treated as a data distribution service.
obstacle: Changing this culture required changing how analysts saw their own role — which is one of the hardest things to do in any organization. These were skilled people who had been rewarded for accuracy and timeliness, not for opinion or interpretation. Asking them to start offering narrative and recommendations felt risky to them — what if they were wrong? What if a leader pushed back? There was also a structural problem: the volume of manual reporting left no time for insight work even if the analysts had wanted to do it.
action: Introduced commentary reporting as a standard expectation — every report now required a written narrative explaining what the numbers were saying, what was notable, and what it implied for decision-making. Built insight reviews as a new cadence — structured sessions where the team presented findings and recommendations rather than data tables. Automated the high-volume, low-value manual reporting work to create time for insight. Coached analysts individually on the craft of strategic storytelling: how to structure a narrative, how to anticipate the reader's question, how to land a recommendation without overreaching. Repositioned the team's identity explicitly — not 'reporting team' but 'insight advisors.'
result: The culture shifted measurably. Leaders began citing team reports in strategic conversations — forwarding them to other executives, referencing them in board materials. Analysts grew in confidence and capability. The team earned the Stars of Excellence Award. Reporting became a strategic input rather than a compliance output.
impact: Culture shifted from reporting to insight advisory; Stars of Excellence Award; analysts became trusted executive advisors
fullStory: The first time I asked the team to write commentary — an actual narrative paragraph explaining what the numbers meant — the room went quiet. One analyst said, 'But what if the leader disagrees with our interpretation?' That was the moment I understood what I was actually dealing with. It wasn't laziness. It was fear — specifically, the fear that having an opinion means being wrong and being judged for it. So I reframed the ask: your commentary isn't a verdict, it's a hypothesis. You're helping the reader think, not thinking for them. Once that landed, the floodgates opened. People started having opinions about the data they'd been staring at for years but had never been allowed to say out loud. The automation work was the enabler — once we took the manual drudgery off their plates, they actually had time to think. The Stars of Excellence Award was the external validation, but the real win was the day I heard a senior leader say in an executive meeting, 'Let me check the insight report — the team usually has something on this.'
notes: Stars of Excellence Award

title: Location Strategy — Reframing Cost Analysis on Marginal Basis
situation: The COO was charged with reviewing Manulife's office and staff location strategy across Toronto, Waterloo, Montreal, Boston, and Atlanta. The CFO team had produced a contribution model comparing average salary costs and rent per person by location, concluding that Atlanta was cheaper than Boston and that the strategy should shift headcount toward Atlanta and Montreal.
obstacle: The analysis had a fundamental flaw: it compared locations on an average basis rather than a marginal basis. Waterloo appeared to be the most expensive Canadian location because average cost per person was high — but the office was only 60% occupied, meaning the fixed costs were being spread over fewer people. More importantly, the analysis treated all office locations the same: Manulife owned the Waterloo and Toronto buildings outright, while in Montreal and Boston they occupied space in buildings Manulife owned but also rented out. Atlanta was a straight rental. These were structurally different cost situations.
action: Had my team reconstruct the analysis on a marginal basis — asking not 'what is the average cost of a person in each location' but 'what is the incremental cost of adding one more person in each location.' In owned buildings with excess capacity, the marginal rent cost was effectively zero — the building cost existed regardless. In nearly-full leased locations like Atlanta and Montreal, adding a person had real incremental rent cost. Layered in wage differences by market. Built a short-term vs long-term framework: in the short term, Waterloo was cheapest (lowest wages, free marginal rent due to vacancy). In the US, Boston was actually preferred over Atlanta in the near term because Boston had excess capacity in an owned building. The long-term recommendation was different: if Manulife could vacate a floor in Boston, the fixed cost saving would be significant.
result: The analysis changed the strategic recommendation entirely — from 'grow Atlanta and Montreal' to 'prioritize Waterloo near-term, focus on Boston floor consolidation for long-term US savings, always prefer Canadian hiring over US on a cost basis.' The COO adopted the revised framework.
impact: Strategic location recommendation reversed; marginal cost framework adopted for future workforce planning
fullStory: The original analysis was a classic average-cost mistake — the kind that looks rigorous on a spreadsheet but breaks down the moment you ask 'what actually changes if we hire one more person here?' When I looked at the Waterloo numbers I immediately saw the problem: of course the average cost per person was high, the building was 60% full. That fixed overhead wasn't going anywhere whether we hired or not. The marginal cost of adding someone to Waterloo was basically just their salary — which happened to be among the lowest of any location. Boston was the same story in reverse: owned building, lots of excess capacity, meaningful opportunity to vacate a floor if we concentrated headcount elsewhere. Atlanta looked cheap until you realized it was the only location where rent was genuinely variable and additional headcount actually increased our real estate bill. I rebuilt the entire model from a marginal lens, and the conclusion flipped. It was a good reminder that the framing of an analysis matters as much as the math inside it.
notes: Strong example of analytical rigor and executive advisory

title: Sales Force Geography — Geospatial Workforce Optimization
situation: A new sales leader had been brought into Canada Retirement and wanted to understand immediately whether the distribution workforce was aligned with where clients and sales opportunities actually existed. His instinct was that there were significant mismatches — regions where the business was over-staffed relative to opportunity and others where coverage gaps were costing deals.
obstacle: Building the right picture required synthesizing data from fundamentally different systems that had never been connected. Client locations came from one system. RVP and RM territories from another. Sales opportunities and RFPs were the hardest: depending on whether an advisor, broker, or consultant was involved, the geography of a deal was recorded differently across Salesforce, compensation systems, and expense reports. No unified dataset existed.
action: Led a team of two analysts from Advanced Analytics to build the integrated dataset. Consolidated client location data, RVP/RM territory maps, and sales opportunity geography by cross-referencing compensation data, RFP logs, and expense reports to determine the actual geographic footprint of each deal. Built a geospatial Power BI dashboard that visualized client concentration against staff coverage across Canada — immediately making visible where high-volume areas were under-served and moderate-volume areas were over-staffed.
result: The new sales leader was able to make structural workforce decisions within weeks of arriving rather than spending a year building intuition. He restructured the distribution team, reducing overall headcount by 15% while measurably improving client coverage and relationship quality in high-concentration markets.
impact: 15% headcount reduction with improved client coverage; distribution workforce restructured within weeks of new leader's arrival
fullStory: The first conversation with the new sales leader was almost comically simple: 'I want to see where our clients are and where our people are. Nobody has been able to show me that.' The data was all there — it was just spread across five different systems with no shared key. The insight work was 80% data engineering: figuring out how to use compensation records and expense data to reconstruct the geography of deals that weren't cleanly tagged in Salesforce. Once we had the map, the answer was obvious. There were regions where we had three RMs covering the same advisors and other regions — high AUM, growing fast — where we had almost nobody. The leader saw it immediately and moved decisively. The 15% headcount reduction sounds dramatic but it was actually coverage-neutral in the markets that mattered — we just redeployed resources from low-value coverage areas to high-value ones. The analytics didn't make the decision for him, but they gave him the confidence to make a big structural move in his first quarter.

title: Building the Insight Engine — From Reporting to Intelligence
situation: The Business Insights & Reporting function I inherited was producing reports — tables of numbers, well-formatted, delivered on time — but not intelligence. Leaders were receiving data and then asking my team what it meant in separate conversations. There was no systematic way to turn analytical output into strategic input. Insight was happening informally, inconsistently, and only when a leader happened to ask the right person.
obstacle: The team was overwhelmed with manual reporting work that consumed most of their capacity. Even if analysts had wanted to do more strategic insight work, there was no time and no defined process for doing it. Beyond capacity, there was no shared definition of what 'insight' even meant at this organization — which meant every analyst was either guessing or defaulting to what was familiar (more data, more charts). There was also the cultural barrier: insight work means having opinions, and having opinions means being exposed to being wrong.
action: Designed the Insight Engine as an operating model — not a technology platform, but a defined system for how insight moves from raw data to executive decision. Defined insight categories (performance insight, risk signal, opportunity signal, behavioral observation) with clear triggers for when each warranted escalation. Automated the manual reporting pipelines to free analyst capacity — this was the prerequisite for everything else. Built governance for insight quality: a review process where insights were tested for validity, significance, and actionability before being surfaced. Created structured insight reviews as a regular cadence. Trained the team on hypothesis-driven analysis, pattern recognition, and executive narrative writing.
result: The function transformed from a reporting team to a strategic intelligence operation. Analysts shifted from spending 80% of their time on manual reporting to spending majority of time on insight generation and advisory work. Leaders began treating the function as a strategic partner rather than a data service — proactively bringing questions to the team rather than just receiving scheduled reports.
impact: Function shifted from report production to strategic advisory; analysts' time on manual reporting fell dramatically; became COO organization's intelligence backbone
fullStory: The Insight Engine was my answer to a problem I'd seen at multiple organizations: smart analytical people buried in manual work, producing reports nobody reads, in a culture where having an opinion feels dangerous. The first thing I did was deal with the capacity problem — we automated everything we could automate, not because automation is cool but because I couldn't ask people to do higher-value work while they were still drowning in low-value work. Once we had time, the next challenge was defining what we were trying to produce. I created a simple taxonomy: what kind of insight is this — performance, risk, opportunity, or behavioural? That framing forced the team to think about the 'so what' before they built anything. The governance piece was critical: every insight went through a review before it reached a leader. Was the finding real or a data artifact? Was it significant enough to act on? What decision does it inform? I still remember the first time a senior leader forwarded one of our insight summaries to the COO with a note saying 'the team found something important here.' That was the moment I knew the engine was running.

title: Crisis Navigation — Maintaining Clarity During Extreme Market Volatility
situation: When COVID-19 hit in March 2020, the retirement business was facing a genuinely novel situation: market collapse, mass workforce transitions, millions of members making emotional financial decisions simultaneously, and leadership teams needing real-time intelligence to manage a business whose key metrics were changing by the day.
obstacle: The existing reporting cadence was built for a stable business cycle — monthly reports, quarterly reviews, annual planning. None of it was equipped for a situation where the numbers were materially different every 48 hours. Leaders were flying blind on the things that mattered most: withdrawal rates, contribution rates, sales pipeline, retention risk. Teams were overwhelmed and distracted. The challenge wasn't just building better dashboards — it was maintaining team cohesion and analytical clarity while everyone was navigating personal disruption.
action: Built a rapid-response insight infrastructure: daily executive briefings covering the KPIs that mattered most in the crisis (withdrawal rates, retention risk indicators, pipeline velocity), a dashboard suite that refreshed daily rather than monthly, and a cross-functional alignment structure so that insights reached the right decision-makers without delays. Applied the employment-based withdrawal model I'd built previously to accurately predict that withdrawals would actually freeze during early COVID — contrary to the instinct of most leaders who expected a surge. Maintained calm and directional clarity with the team, setting clear priorities and shielding them from escalating demands that would have fragmented focus.
result: Leadership made faster, more informed decisions throughout the crisis period. The withdrawal freeze prediction proved accurate — avoiding the over-reaction that would have damaged the business. The team held together and performed at a high level. Trust in the insights function deepened significantly as a result of its performance during the period of highest stress.
impact: Prevented major forecast misses during COVID; real-time intelligence enabled faster executive decisions during crisis
fullStory: In March 2020 I had a phone call with our CFO at 9pm on a Sunday. He wanted to know what our withdrawal rates were going to do. Every instinct in the room was saying 'massive surge' — markets were collapsing, people were scared, surely everyone was going to pull their money out. I told him I thought the opposite would happen: when people lose their jobs, they stop contributing — but they also stop paying attention to their accounts. The withdrawals would freeze, not surge. That prediction came from research I'd done years earlier on the employment market as the real driver of member behavior, not short-term market returns. When the data started coming in and I was right, it fundamentally changed how the leadership team thought about what analytical capability actually meant. The crisis was also a management challenge — keeping a team producing high-quality work under extreme time pressure while they themselves were dealing with personal upheaval. I made the priorities explicit: these five metrics matter most right now, everything else waits. That clarity was what got us through.
notes: Strong COVID-era crisis leadership story with specific prediction

title: Strategic Alignment — Unifying Conflicting Priorities
situation: Multiple teams with interdependent workstreams were operating from incompatible priority lists. The Insights function needed data infrastructure changes from the Technology team, who had their own roadmap. Product needed reporting capabilities that Insights was building, but with different design assumptions. Finance needed forecasting models that Analytics was developing, but with conflicting definitions. Everyone was working hard and nothing was connecting.
obstacle: Each team had legitimate competing pressures — their own leadership priorities, their own performance objectives, their own timelines. The tension wasn't caused by bad faith. It was caused by a structural misalignment: no shared framework for sequencing work across teams. Historical tensions between some of the groups had created a default of avoiding joint conversations rather than having them.
action: Facilitated a structured alignment series — not a single all-hands meeting but a deliberate sequence of bilateral conversations first, then a combined session. Mapped interdependencies visually so teams could see how their work either enabled or blocked others. Translated strategic goals into shared operational objectives with specific handoffs and sequencing logic. Built a unified roadmap that each team could own a section of — with clear accountability for the intersections. Established a recurring monthly alignment forum to keep the momentum going.
result: Teams that had been working in productive friction started working in productive coordination. Delivery velocity on cross-functional initiatives improved noticeably. Several initiatives that had been stalled for months unblocked within weeks.
impact: Cross-functional delivery velocity improved; multiple stalled initiatives unblocked
fullStory: What I've learned about cross-functional misalignment is that it almost never comes from people who don't want to work together — it comes from people who haven't had the right structure for doing it. The teams in this situation were all competent, all motivated, all frustrated. They'd had joint meetings before that went nowhere because everyone showed up defending their roadmap. I changed the sequence: I talked to each team separately first, not to gather intelligence but to genuinely understand what they needed and what was blocking them. Then I built the interdependency map. When I showed it to the combined group, something shifted — you could see the moment people realized that their blocker was the same as someone else's dependency. From there the alignment conversation almost ran itself. The recurring forum was the part most organizations skip — the reason alignment initiatives fail isn't the workshop, it's the six weeks after the workshop when everyone drifts back to their own priorities.

title: Insight Reporting Operating Model — Fixing Execution Failure
situation: As the Business Insights & Reporting function grew and took on larger, more complex reports involving multiple teams — the distribution team, product, finance, analytics — execution started breaking down. Reports were consistently delivered late. Key meetings were getting rescheduled or missed. Team members were showing up underprepared because the working group for each report had unclear membership and undefined roles. Every major report felt like managing a new project from scratch with a team that had never worked together.
obstacle: The problem wasn't individual capability — it was a structural one. Working groups for each report had been assembled informally. People joined because they were interested, not because they had a defined role and a real commitment. The touchpoint meetings were scheduled reactively, which meant they were constantly being moved when someone had a conflict. There was no shared mental model of 'how a report gets made' — so every report reinvented the process.
action: Ran a half-day offsite with the core Insights Reporting team to diagnose the problem through two lenses: what does winning feel like, and what gets in the way of winning? From that conversation, designed a 6-week insight reporting operating model. The model defined three distinct groups for every report: an oversight group (decision-making authority), a working group (delivery responsibility — with the explicit rule that everyone in the working group had to have a job, not just an interest), and an audience (who the report was for and what decision it should inform). Every touchpoint meeting was booked on day one of the project — no more reactive scheduling. One of the most counterintuitive design choices: we held the first results review meeting before any results existed, using it to align on what the report needed to show and why.
result: Piloted the model on the Participant Outcomes Index launch report. Delivered exactly on the committed date — the first major cross-functional report to do so in recent memory. The model became the standard for all major insight reports going forward and was adopted beyond the immediate team. The report itself has been referenced and shared more than two dozen times since its publication.
impact: Insight reporting process standardized; Participant Outcomes Index report delivered on time; model adopted across the organization
fullStory: The half-day offsite was the turning point. I asked a simple question: describe what it feels like when a report goes well. The answers were all about clarity — clear roles, clear timelines, clear decisions. Then I asked what gets in the way. The answers were predictable but important: ambiguous membership, last-minute meeting rescheduling, showing up to a working session without knowing what your job is. So we designed a process that systematically eliminated every one of those failure modes. The most surprising insight from the offsite was the idea of holding the first results meeting before you have results — it sounds backwards, but it forces the team to think about what insight they're trying to generate before they're deep in the data and too attached to what they've built to change course. When we piloted it on the Participant Outcomes Index and hit our delivery date, the reaction from stakeholders was almost disbelief. Reports don't come on time. This one did, and it was because the process was clean.
notes: Strong process design story with specific outcome

title: Planning Model Redesign — Building a Driver-Based Forecast
situation: The annual planning model was a large Excel workbook that few people fully understood and fewer trusted. It had been built incrementally over many years by many hands, with manual inputs at every stage. The process of producing a forecast required weeks of analyst time just to populate the inputs, and the outputs were routinely questioned by business leaders who couldn't trace a number back to its underlying assumption. Forecast accuracy was poor and variance explanations consumed significant senior time.
obstacle: Rebuilding the model from scratch meant convincing the business that the pain of transition was worth it — specifically, that the new model would be more accurate, more transparent, and faster to run. FP&A teams and regional CFOs had deep institutional knowledge embedded in the old model's quirks, and there was a real fear that a new model would lose that context. The business had also never used a true driver-based approach, so there was no existing template to point to.
action: Mapped the key economic and behavioral drivers of each planning input — revenue by source, expenses by category, member flow assumptions — replacing static line items with statistical and economic relationships. Built a driver-based architecture where the key inputs were things like employment rates, contribution rates by segment, and market return assumptions, with all other lines derived from those. Automated the data ingestion to eliminate manual inputs. Built scenario capabilities so planning cycles could generate multiple views (base, stress, upside) without manual rework. Partnered with FP&A and business leaders at every stage to validate assumptions and ensure the model reflected how the business actually worked.
result: Forecast accuracy improved significantly. Planning cycle time dropped substantially as manual input work was eliminated. Leaders could trace any output number back through the model's logic, which dramatically reduced variance explanation time. The scenario capability made strategic planning conversations richer.
impact: Forecast accuracy improved; planning cycle time reduced substantially; driver-based model adopted for strategic planning
fullStory: The old planning model was essentially a budgeting spreadsheet pretending to be a forecast. Every line was an assumption someone had typed in — either last year's actual plus a growth rate someone had picked, or a number that had been there for so long nobody remembered where it came from. When I started mapping the real drivers of the business, the model almost wrote itself: contribution rates follow employment, withdrawals are driven by job transitions, net revenue is a function of retention and new business. If you build the model around those relationships, you don't have to guess at the lines — you just update the macro assumptions and the numbers flow. The resistance to rebuilding was real, but I found that the easiest way through it was to run the old model and new model side by side on the same historical period. The new model's accuracy was demonstrably better. That made the conversation much easier.

title: Withdrawal Forecasting — Discovering Employment Drives Behavior, Not Market Returns
situation: The retirement business had been forecasting member withdrawal rates based on the intuitive assumption that short-term market returns drove behavior — that when markets were down, members would panic and withdraw, and when markets were up, they'd stay. Planning cycles were built around this assumption. Every time the market moved significantly, the business would model a corresponding change in withdrawal rates.
obstacle: The assumption seemed logical but was generating consistently inaccurate forecasts. The business couldn't explain why withdrawal rates during certain market downturns were lower than expected, or why they surged during a period of strong market performance. The conventional wisdom was so embedded that questioning it required not just a different hypothesis but a compelling analytical case that would hold up to challenge from the CFO and chief economist.
action: Undertook systematic research with the Chief Economist, analyzing correlations between historical withdrawal rates and a range of potential drivers: short-term market returns, long-term market returns, employment rates, economic cycle indicators, and member age demographics. The finding was counterintuitive but statistically robust: withdrawals were driven primarily by employment market conditions, not short-term market performance. When people have jobs and income security, they don't touch their retirement accounts — even when markets are volatile. When they transition jobs or become unemployed, they're more likely to access their accounts. Rebuilt the forecasting model around employment-based drivers and applied it through COVID and the Great Resignation.
result: Forecast accuracy improved dramatically. During COVID, applied the model to correctly predict that withdrawal rates would freeze rather than surge — contrary to the initial expectations of most leaders. During the Great Resignation period of full employment, predicted the subsequent withdrawal increase. Both predictions proved accurate and prevented major forecast misses and avoided ELT-level escalations.
impact: Withdrawal model accurately predicted COVID freeze and Great Resignation surge; prevented major forecast misses at critical decision points
fullStory: Everyone expected COVID to trigger a withdrawal surge. Markets had dropped 30% in weeks, people were scared, and the conventional wisdom — which was that market performance drove member behavior — pointed to a flood of withdrawals. I thought the opposite was likely, and I had the data to back it. The employment-based model showed that people in work, even uncertain work, tend to leave their retirement accounts alone. It's when they lose income that accounts become attractive as emergency resources. In early COVID, employment was still technically high — the layoffs hadn't hit yet. So I predicted a freeze, not a surge. When I walked the CFO through that prediction in March 2020, I could see the skepticism. But I laid out the historical correlations and the mechanism. The data came in over the next six weeks and confirmed it. That was a career-defining analytical moment — not because I was lucky, but because I had built a framework grounded in the right causal logic. The Great Resignation was the sequel: full employment, so withdrawal rates would be lower than expected. Both times the model was right. Both times it changed how the business prepared.
notes: One of the most intellectually distinctive analytical stories

title: Participant Conversion Funnel — Reach, Not Conversion, Was the Problem
situation: The Member Retention team was focused entirely on conversion — improving the rate at which members who called in or visited the website ultimately chose to roll over or stay in the plan. All the coaching, scripting, and process improvement was aimed at the moment of contact. The sole performance metric was capture rate: of the terminating members we touched, how many did we keep?
obstacle: Capture rate was a useful metric but it was measuring the wrong constraint. Improving conversion at the bottom of the funnel didn't matter if the top of the funnel was too small. Nobody had ever built a full picture of the termination-to-outcome journey, so the team was optimizing a step they could measure while ignoring steps they couldn't.
action: Built a complete participant conversion funnel from termination to final outcome — the first time this had been done in the business. Started with the full universe of active and inactive members who terminated or retired in a given period. Mapped how many transacted before the business was aware they were leaving. Tracked contact attempts across email, outbound calls, inbound calls, and digital. Measured how many were actually reached, how many engaged in a conversation, and then at each subsequent stage what proportion chose each outcome — staying in plan, moving to another plan, rolling over to the Manulife product, cashing out, or taking no action. The finding was striking: the conversion rate among members who were actually engaged was reasonably strong. The leak was at the top of the funnel — the business was simply not reaching enough terminating members to matter.
result: The strategy shifted entirely from conversion optimization to reach expansion — moving from 'how do we convince people who call us' to 'how do we identify and contact more people before they decide.' This reframing drove a 25% improvement in participant retention and became the basis for rebranding the team from 'Member Retention' to 'Participant Outcomes.'
impact: 25% improvement in participant retention; strategy shifted from conversion to reach; team rebranded from Member Retention to Participant Outcomes
fullStory: I built the funnel because I had a suspicion: the team was working hard on the wrong problem. The capture rate looked reasonable — when we actually got someone on the phone, we retained them at a decent clip. But the number of people we were getting on the phone in the first place was tiny relative to the total universe of terminating members. It was like trying to improve a leaking bucket by making the bottom plug tighter when most of the water was leaking out the side. When I showed the funnel to the head of the team, there was a long pause. She said, 'So we're actually good at conversion, we're just not talking to enough people.' Yes. Exactly. The strategy pivot was almost instantaneous — because once you see the funnel clearly, the priority becomes obvious. The 25% retention improvement came not from better scripts or better training but from systematically building the top of the funnel: better identification of at-risk members, better multi-channel outreach, better early intervention triggers. The renaming from Member Retention to Participant Outcomes was partly symbolic but partly real — once you stop thinking of the job as 'retain the people who call us,' you start thinking about outcomes across the full journey.
notes: Strong story about analytical reframing changing strategic direction

title: NPS Valuation Model — Quantifying the Financial Value of Experience
situation: The NPS (Net Promoter Score) was tracked and reported, but it existed in a separate world from financial planning. When the advice and member experience teams requested investment, finance would ask for an ROI calculation — and nobody could produce one. The assumption was that NPS impact was impossible to isolate given all the other variables affecting retention, contribution rates, and AUM growth.
obstacle: The 'we can't isolate NPS impact' argument was used to perpetually deprioritize experience investment in favor of initiatives with cleaner financial cases. The challenge wasn't just analytical — it was political. Teams who stood to benefit from NPS investment had been unable to make the financial case. Finance was appropriately skeptical of any model that assigned a dollar value to a soft metric. The model had to be rigorous enough to survive challenge from the CFO.
action: Built a valuation model with the finance team that connected NPS to each of the key value drivers in the business. Ran structured hypothesis tests across data, marketing, CX, and sales to isolate the incremental impact of higher NPS on retention rates, contribution rates, rollover capture, and referral behavior. Controlled for confounding variables through cohort analysis — comparing members with similar plan characteristics and demographics who had received different service experiences. Converted the findings into an annual earnings equivalent, showing that a 10-point NPS improvement drove approximately a 5% increase in enterprise value.
result: NPS became a financially credible KPI for the first time — with a defensible dollar value attached to every point of improvement. The model directly unlocked investment for the advice platform by providing the ROI case that had previously been impossible to produce. Experience was repositioned from a cost centre to a revenue driver.
impact: 10-point NPS improvement = ~5% enterprise value increase; advice platform investment unlocked; NPS repositioned as financial KPI
fullStory: The NPS valuation problem is one of the most interesting analytical challenges I've worked on, because the conventional wisdom — that you can't isolate the impact of a soft metric — is partly right and partly an excuse. You can't run a controlled experiment in a live business. But you can do what I did: find natural experiments in the data. Members who had similar characteristics but different service experiences. Plans that went through a service disruption versus those that didn't. Advisor relationships that deepened versus those that atrophied. When you start stacking those comparisons, a consistent pattern emerges. The 10-point NPS to 5% enterprise value relationship wasn't a point estimate I invented — it was the converging finding from four different analytical approaches. The CFO challenge was real. He pushed hard on the methodology. But I'd built it knowing he would, and the model held. The downstream impact — actually getting investment approved for the advice platform — was the payoff.
notes: Distinctive financial analytics story

title: Cost of Capital Model — CFO Advisory at OMERS
situation: As the internal analytics lead for Corporate Finance FP&A at OMERS, I was asked to support the CFO in evaluating capital allocation decisions across a $105B pension portfolio. The fundamental challenge was that different investment teams used different discount rates and cost of capital assumptions — meaning the same investment opportunity would look attractive or unattractive depending on which team evaluated it. The CFO had no unified standard.
obstacle: Building a unified cost of capital framework for a pension fund is analytically complex: unlike a corporation, a pension fund's cost of capital is fundamentally about its liability structure — the required return needed to meet obligations — rather than a weighted average of debt and equity costs. Different teams used different interpretations, and each had a defensible position. Getting investment leadership to adopt a single framework meant navigating both the analytical complexity and the political reality that some teams' preferred investments would look worse under a unified standard.
action: Built a comprehensive cost of capital model from first principles, starting with the liability structure and required return of the pension fund. Integrated market data, risk premiums, duration-matched benchmarks, and portfolio characteristics. Created scenario-based outputs showing cost of capital under different liability assumptions and market regimes. Documented all assumptions explicitly so the model could be challenged and improved over time. Presented the model directly to the CFO, walking through the logic, the assumptions, and the sensitivity to key inputs.
result: The CFO adopted the model as the enterprise standard for capital allocation decisions and ALM (asset-liability management). It became the foundational framework for evaluating investment proposals and strategic capital allocation — moving the organization from a fragmented set of individual team assumptions to a single, defensible standard.
impact: Enterprise cost of capital model adopted as OMERS standard for capital allocation and ALM
fullStory: Building a cost of capital model for a pension fund is one of those problems where the intellectual challenge and the political challenge are equally significant. The analytical work was genuinely interesting — a pension fund's cost of capital is anchored to its liability obligations in a way that corporate finance models don't capture well. You have to think about duration, about inflation sensitivity, about the compounding effect of underfunding. I enjoyed that part. The harder part was the CFO presentation. He had seen a lot of models come across his desk and had a healthy skepticism for anything that looked like it was designed to justify a conclusion rather than arrive at one. So I built the model to be as transparent as possible — every assumption was labeled, documented, and stress-tested in the scenario analysis. When he pushed on a specific assumption during the presentation, I could show him exactly what changed if he was right. That transparency was what earned his confidence. When he said he'd adopt it as the enterprise standard, he was saying more about his trust in the methodology than his agreement with any specific number.

title: Required Return + Duration Framework — Reframing Pension Risk
situation: The CIO at OMERS was presenting a portfolio narrative built around the effective cash yield metric — a measure that showed strong and stable income generation from the fixed income portfolio. The narrative was being used to support strategic capital allocation decisions and was the primary lens through which investment performance was evaluated at the senior level.
obstacle: The effective cash yield metric was obscuring significant structural risk. The portfolio carried large long CDS positions that created a large negative effective duration — meaning the portfolio was actually highly sensitive to interest rate movements in a way the cash yield metric didn't capture. Combined with a levered beta above one from concentrated large-cap equity exposure, the portfolio's true risk profile was materially different from what the income-focused narrative suggested. Challenging the CIO's preferred metric — as a relatively junior analyst operating as an outsider providing independent oversight — risked significant political fallout.
action: Built an independent expected return framework grounded in total return analysis rather than income metrics. Calculated weighted beta and modified duration to surface the true risk profile of the portfolio. Identified the negative effective duration from large long CDS positions and the leveraged equity risk — two factors completely invisible in the cash yield framework. Prepared a clear presentation of the alternative view and brought it directly to the CFO, framing it as a complementary lens rather than a refutation of the CIO's narrative, but being explicit about the risk implications.
result: Reframed internal evaluation of capital markets performance. Leadership gained a more complete view of portfolio risk and structural vulnerabilities that had been masked by income-focused reporting. The CFO's understanding of the true return and risk profile of the portfolio shifted materially.
impact: Capital markets risk framework reframed at CFO level; hidden duration and leverage risk surfaced and addressed
fullStory: My role at OMERS was explicitly to provide independent oversight of the capital markets portfolio — reporting directly to the CFO rather than to the investment teams. That independence was both the most valuable thing I brought and the most politically complicated. When I identified that the CIO's preferred cash yield narrative was masking a large negative duration position and levered beta above one, I had a choice: say nothing and stay comfortable, or surface it and risk the political fallout. I surfaced it. The approach mattered enormously: I didn't walk into the CFO's office and say 'the CIO is wrong.' I built an alternative framework that showed the total return and risk picture alongside the income picture, and let the comparison speak for itself. The CFO was analytically sophisticated enough to see what I was showing him. The conversation about what to do with that information was his to have with the investment leadership — my job was to make sure he had the full picture.
notes: Strong capital markets and political navigation story

title: Concession Efficiency Analytics — Reducing Pricing Lapses from 4% to 1.5%
situation: Sponsor retention was suffering from pricing-related lapses — clients leaving because their repricing (concession) wasn't working as retention tool. The quarterly Excel reporting that existed on concession efficacy consistently showed that repriced clients were actually more likely to lapse than clients who hadn't received a concession. This was producing a paradox: distribution leaders knew from experience that concessions worked, but the data said they didn't. Finance was increasingly resistant to approving concession budgets.
obstacle: The existing reporting was built on a fundamentally flawed calculation method — grouping concessions by program and year, which created selection bias and measurement timing problems. The real dynamics of concession effectiveness were hidden inside the data but the old farm-to-table Excel process made it practically impossible to test alternative approaches. Rebuilding the analysis from scratch required both the new AIR data infrastructure and the willingness to question a methodology that had been in place for years.
action: Used the AIR platform to rebuild the concession analysis from first principles, testing multiple hypotheses about what variable actually predicted retention post-concession. After ruling out program-type, deal size, and year-of-concession, identified the critical variable: time since last concession. Clients who received a new concession within a year of their last one had strong retention. Clients who went without a renewal were highly likely to lapse the following year. Discovered this effect was present but different by geography: in the US, the beneficial effect of a concession lasted approximately one year; in Canada, it extended to two years. Also found that automated repricing strategies had minimal impact; RM-discretionary repricing was significantly more effective.
result: US pricing-related lapses fell from 4% of the block annually to 1.5% and never rose above 2% again. The analysis also changed the concession strategy: moved to more frequent, smaller RM-discretionary concessions rather than the previous model of large periodic automated programs.
impact: Pricing-related lapses fell from 4% to 1.5%; never rose above 2% again; concession strategy redesigned
fullStory: This is one of my favorite analytical stories because it demonstrates exactly why data infrastructure matters. The previous Excel-based analysis showed that concessions made clients more likely to leave — a result that everyone knew was wrong from experience but nobody could prove wrong analytically. The problem was the calculation method: when you group by program and look at a 12-month window, you're measuring the population of at-risk clients who received concessions, not the counterfactual impact of the concession itself. It's like trying to measure whether hospitals make you healthier by looking at what percentage of patients in hospitals are sick. Of course repriced clients lapse at a higher rate — they were identified as lapse risks before they received the concession. The insight that mattered was: given that they received a concession, how long did it work? That's the question the AIR let me ask, because I could track each client's concession history over time. The one-year US, two-year Canada finding was operationally actionable. The RM-discretionary finding was perhaps even more important — it told us that the human judgment element in repricing was significantly more effective than automated rules. Which is the opposite of what most technology-first organizations want to hear, but it was the data.
notes: 4% → 1.5% pricing-related lapses. One of the most quantitatively impactful stories.

title: AIR Platform — $20B Net Sales Transformation
situation: US Retirement was consistently losing more business than it was winning — net sales were averaging negative $20B USD per year. Distribution leaders were working with a basic Sales ($ total) metric and a fundamentally broken close ratio measure. The close ratio calculation was so mathematically unstable — dependent on the mix of large and small opportunities in a given period — that the conversation in every review meeting was about why the metric looked the way it did, not about what was actually happening commercially.
obstacle: The close ratio problem was structural: with 100,000 small plan opportunities and only 10 large ones in a year, the count-based metric was measuring small plan activity. The dollar-based metric swung violently based on whether a few jumbo plans happened to open in the window. Neither version told you whether the business was improving at winning. Beyond the metric, there was no integrated view of the distribution business — sales, retention, pricing, advisor relationships, pipeline velocity all lived in separate systems and separate reports, none connected to each other.
action: Built AIR — the Analytics, Insights & Reporting platform — integrating sales, retention, pricing, advisor relationships, pipeline, and segment data for the first time. Replaced the close ratio with the win rate: Wins / (Wins + Losses), settled on a closed-deal basis, calculable by dollar, count, or net annual revenue, and sliceable by segment, RVP, division, region, industry, and advisor. Added pipeline velocity metrics (new opportunities by week, courtship period — time from open to closed won), segment analysis using purchased industry data to show market share by advisor type and plan size, and concession efficiency analytics. Automated all reporting and built interactive Power BI dashboards that distribution leaders could navigate themselves.
result: US Retirement net sales went from consistently negative $20B to net zero by 2024. New opportunities grew 30% on a count basis and 50%+ on a dollar basis from 2018 to 2022. Pricing-related lapses fell from 4% to 1.5%. Global sponsor lapse rate improved from 8% to under 6%. Distribution leaders shifted from reactive management to proactive intelligence — with specific advisors, segments, and regions to invest in or defend.
impact: $20B+ improvement in US net sales; lapse rate 8%→6%; new opportunities +30-50%; pricing lapses 4%→1.5%
fullStory: The close ratio problem was the starting point, but AIR became much bigger than fixing a metric. When I dug into why the close ratio was broken, I realized the underlying issue was that nobody actually knew what was happening commercially at any useful level of granularity. You could see total sales. You could see total lapses. But you couldn't see which advisors you were winning and losing with, which plan sizes were your strongest segment, why a specific RVP had a great quarter while another one didn't. AIR was built to answer those questions. The win rate fix sounds simple — just change the denominator from opportunities opened to deals closed — but it required building a data model that connected Salesforce data to compensation records to get clean 'won' and 'lost' outcomes. Once that existed, every other metric became possible. The courtship period metric — tracking how long deals sat in the pipeline — was one of the most impactful in terms of behavior change. Simply being able to see which RVP's deals closed fastest created enormous incentive to move faster. The distribution team's transformation from reactive to proactive wasn't a training program. It was a data infrastructure investment.
notes: The flagship commercial impact story — $20B net sales improvement

title: Forecasting Through Crisis — Predicting COVID and Great Resignation Flows
situation: COVID-19 created an unprecedented forecasting challenge for the retirement business in March 2020. Markets had collapsed 30% in weeks. Millions of members were navigating economic uncertainty. Leadership expected a significant surge in withdrawals as members accessed their retirement savings in a crisis. The business was preparing for a large negative impact to AUM and cash flows.
obstacle: Every conventional forecast pointed to a withdrawal surge. Market-driven withdrawal models predicted it. Leadership instinct predicted it. The financial press was predicting it. Arguing against consensus required a framework that could withstand challenge from sophisticated executives and a CFO who had seen a lot of crisis forecasts go wrong.
action: Applied the employment-based withdrawal model I had built in prior years. The model showed that withdrawals were driven by job transitions and employment security, not short-term market performance. In early COVID, employment was technically still high — mass layoffs had not yet materialized. The model predicted a withdrawal freeze, not a surge: members in employment would leave their accounts alone even in volatile markets. Presented this counter-consensus prediction to the CFO in March 2020, laid out the analytical basis, and committed to the forecast. Also applied the model to the subsequent Great Resignation period: full employment, therefore lower withdrawal rates than fear-driven models would suggest.
result: The COVID freeze prediction proved accurate. Withdrawal rates declined rather than surging in Q2 2020. The Great Resignation prediction also held — withdrawal rates during the full-employment period were significantly lower than competitors with market-driven models expected. Both predictions prevented major forecast misses and the over-reaction strategies that would have followed incorrect forecasts.
impact: COVID withdrawal freeze correctly predicted; Great Resignation flows correctly predicted; major forecast misses avoided at critical business decision points
fullStory: March 2020, Sunday evening, CFO on the phone wanting to know what our withdrawal rates would do. I told him I expected them to freeze. There was a silence. The whole world was expecting a surge. Markets were down 30%, people were scared, all the intuition pointed to massive withdrawals. My argument was simple: people don't raid their retirement accounts while they're employed. They do it when they lose income. Employment was still technically high — the layoff wave hadn't arrived yet. When it did, then we'd see withdrawals. But right now? People were anxious, but they were still getting paychecks. The analytical underpinning was years of work I'd done on what actually drives withdrawal behavior. When the data came in over April and May, I was right. And the Great Resignation was the sequel two years later — full employment, therefore withdrawals lower than expected. The value of that work wasn't just the accuracy. It was that the business made better decisions — managed its cash positions correctly, didn't over-react to the market noise, positioned its planning assumptions on reality rather than fear.
notes: COVID prediction story — counter-consensus and correct

title: Revenue Framework Redesign — Diversified Revenue Architecture
situation: Revenue reporting for the Retirement business was built around a single revenue line that blended together fundamentally different revenue streams — rollover revenue, recurring contribution-based revenue, fee income, and advice revenue. The aggregated number masked the underlying health of the business: a great period of new sales could be offset by retention losses, and neither the growth nor the retention problem would be visible in the top-line number.
obstacle: Disaggregating the revenue required agreement on definitions across multiple regional finance teams who had developed their own categorizations over time. There was also a cultural challenge: some leaders preferred the aggregate number because it was easier to manage narratives around. Switching to a diversified framework would surface performance dimensions that had previously been invisible — and some of those dimensions would not look flattering.
action: Identified the key revenue streams that required separate visibility: new business revenue, retention revenue, contribution growth revenue, advice and ancillary revenue, and pricing-impacted revenue. Built a diversified revenue architecture with clear definitions agreed across regional CFOs. Integrated the framework into the planning model and monthly reporting cycle. Partnered with the CFO and regional finance leads to get buy-in on the definitions and to build the first set of historical comparisons under the new framework.
result: ELT gained visibility into the health of the business at a level of granularity that had not previously existed. Revenue conversations became more actionable — leadership could see whether a good period was driven by strong new sales, strong retention, or simply pricing improvements. Strategic prioritization sharpened as a result.
impact: Diversified revenue framework adopted; ELT revenue conversations became actionable rather than aggregate
fullStory: The aggregate revenue number is a management comfort blanket — it lets you feel like you're monitoring the business without actually seeing it. Good new sales can hide terrible retention. Pricing increases can mask contribution volume decline. I wanted leadership to see the moving parts, not just the total. The political challenge was real: some people liked the aggregate number precisely because it was ambiguous. Surfacing the components meant surfacing problems that had been invisible. I handled this by building the historical picture first — showing what the disaggregated numbers looked like in previous periods and what stories they told that the aggregate had obscured. Once leaders could see what they'd been missing, the argument for the new framework was self-evident.

title: Building GRID — Enterprise Single Source of Truth
situation: When I took over the Business Insights & Reporting function in 2022, the data landscape for Global W&AM was fragmented across multiple business units, regions, and systems. Canada, US, and Asia each maintained their own reporting infrastructure with their own definitions, processes, and output formats. The consequence was that any cross-regional leadership conversation started with a version of 'which number is right' — and the answer was always context-dependent. Leaders had stopped trusting the numbers they were receiving.
obstacle: The fragmentation wasn't accidental — it had been built intentionally. Each regional team had genuine reasons for their approach. Regional finance teams had built reporting optimized for their own decision-making context. Consolidating without destroying what worked required significant diplomatic work alongside the technical work. There was also a political dimension: 'owning' the enterprise single source of truth is a position of significant organizational influence, and several teams had a stake in how that played out.
action: Built GRID as the enterprise reporting and intelligence platform for Global W&AM — not just a data warehouse but a designed insight delivery system. Unified data sources across regions and business lines, standardized metric definitions and calculation logic, and built the infrastructure to deliver insight rather than raw data. Partnered with stakeholders across the COO, CFO, and regional finance organizations at every stage to ensure GRID reflected how the business actually worked and to build genuine buy-in. Established governance processes so the platform could evolve without breaking existing reporting.
result: GRID became the undisputed single source of truth across Global W&AM, supporting the $800B+ AUM platform including Private Markets. The recurring 'which number is right' conversation largely disappeared from senior leadership meetings. Cross-regional strategic decisions became faster and better grounded because leadership was working from a shared analytical foundation.
impact: Enterprise single source of truth established for $800B+ Global W&AM platform; eliminated cross-regional reporting fragmentation
fullStory: GRID is the project I'm most proud of, and not because it was technically impressive — it was. But the harder achievement was organizational. Building a genuine single source of truth requires convincing multiple teams that they're better off with a shared standard than with their own system. You can't compel that. People will route around a mandate. I spent months before writing a single line of data code building relationships and understanding — what does the distribution team need to see, what does the CFO care about, where does the current reporting fall short? I made sure that GRID was designed to solve problems people actually had, not problems I thought they should have. When regional teams started using GRID as their primary reporting source by choice — not because I'd switched anything off — that was the validation moment. The definition of 'single source of truth' isn't a technical achievement. It's when people stop asking 'is this number right' and start asking 'what does it mean.'
notes: Flagship data infrastructure story

title: RM Dashboard — AI-Powered Next-Best-Action Engine
situation: Relationship managers were managing large books of business with limited tools for prioritization. They had access to some client data but it was fragmented across systems — retention risk signals in one place, product utilization in another, pipeline in Salesforce, concession history elsewhere. The result was reactive relationship management: RMs responded to clients who called, rather than proactively reaching out to clients who needed attention.
obstacle: Building an integrated RM dashboard required connecting data sources that had never been joined at the client level. The AI component — embedding predictive analytics and next-best-action prompts directly in the Power BI interface — required both data infrastructure work and a governance model for how algorithmic recommendations would be validated and updated. There was also a change management challenge: RMs would only use the tool if it surfaced genuinely useful signals, not noise.
action: Built the RM Dashboard by consolidating all client-level data from GRID — performance, retention, product utilization, client features, pricing history, and advisor relationships — into a unified client view. Added the client features dataset: for each client, which features they were eligible for, which they subscribed to, and which they could still activate. Embedded Copilot-powered next-best-action prompts directly in the dashboard using predictive lapse risk models, cross-sell readiness indicators, and advice utilization scores. Designed the dashboard to be customizable by role: distribution leads saw sales and retention metrics, CFOs saw financial performance and pricing impact, individual RMs saw their specific book of business. Built a feedback loop where RM recommendations could be captured and fed back into the underlying model.
result: The RM Dashboard became a daily tool across the distribution function. RMs shifted from reactive client management to proactive outreach. The next-best-action prompts surfaced specific, timely opportunities that RMs could act on immediately — lapse risk alerts, cross-sell windows, advice utilization gaps. The feedback loop began building an organizational intelligence asset that would improve over time.
impact: Distribution shifted to proactive management; AI-powered next-best-action prompts embedded in daily workflow; feedback loop built organizational intelligence over time
fullStory: The vision for the RM Dashboard was simple: give every RM the same intelligence advantage that the best RMs build intuitively over years of experience, but make it available on day one and keep it updated in real time. The AI component was the part most people got excited about, but the harder work was the data foundation — joining client performance, pricing history, product features, and advisor relationships in a way that was clean enough to trust. We were meticulous about what triggered a next-best-action prompt: it had to be specific ('this client's match rate has declined for three consecutive quarters'), actionable ('contact by end of next month — their contract review is coming up'), and right often enough that RMs would trust it. The customization by role was important. A distribution VP and an individual RM are looking for completely different things from the same data. I didn't want to build three dashboards — I wanted one platform with intelligent defaults that adapted to who was using it. The feedback loop was the part I was most excited about: every recommendation an RM accepts or rejects is a training signal for the model. That's how organizational intelligence compounds.
notes: Leading-edge AI-in-the-workflow story

title: Data Lake Governance — Resolving a 6-Month Access Impasse in 48 Hours
situation: The CDO organization had introduced new rules distinguishing between 'Production' and non-production EDL environments, driven by a political dispute between the CDO team and the Advanced Analytics team over data ownership. The new rules meant that data stored in the Advanced Analytics EDL space could only be used for internal purposes — not for any client-facing output. A report used by distribution RMs to support plan design reviews with clients had been built years earlier using Advanced Analytics data sources and suddenly stopped working. My director Tom had been trying to get it fixed for six months.
obstacle: The situation had calcified into a political standoff. Tom had been sending requests and getting partial responses for six months. The head of Advanced Analytics — Ben — understood the political risk to his team if they were caught circumventing the CDO's new rules. He had effectively gone silent, offering workarounds that never materialized and avoiding direct conversation. A complete rebuild of the solution would take months and funding that would be difficult to obtain. The actual fix required someone with authority to grant permission — and nobody had taken ownership of doing that.
action: Identified the likely root cause through a casual conversation with the VP of the CDO team — the new PROD vs non-PROD rules, and their unintended impact on the Advanced Analytics-dependent report. Set up a meeting with Ben, the data lead at Advanced Analytics. He didn't attend. Set up another meeting. He didn't attend again. Sent him a direct IM acknowledging openly that he appeared to be avoiding the meeting — making the human dynamic explicit in a way that was honest but not confrontational. Ben responded immediately, apologized, and offered time. Verified the meeting the day before to prevent last-minute cancellation. In the meeting, understood Ben's position: he couldn't provide the data without authorization because the risk to his team was too high. Asked one reframing question: 'Who needs to sign off on this for you to feel comfortable continuing to provide this data until we can rebuild?' Within 30 minutes of that conversation, had email authorization from both my boss (the COO) and the VP of the CDO team, with Ben and his manager copied. The data access was restored the next day.
result: A six-month impasse resolved in 48 hours. The client-facing report was restored without requiring a full rebuild. The reframing from 'can you help us' to 'who needs to authorize this so you're protected' was the move that unlocked it.
impact: 6-month data access impasse resolved in 48 hours; client-facing report restored without costly rebuild
fullStory: The Ben situation is one of the clearest examples I have of how organizational problems masquerade as technical ones. Tom had been sending the right requests, to the right people, with the right documentation, for six months. Nothing moved. That's not a technical problem. That's an incentive problem. Ben wasn't stonewalling because he was unhelpful — he was protecting his team from a political risk that nobody had offered to take off his hands.
