Once I understood that, the fix was obvious: I just needed to make his risk someone else's problem. The question 'who needs to sign off for you to feel comfortable' did all the work. It shifted the frame from 'will you help us' — which put all the accountability on Ben — to 'let me get you the cover you need.' The IM calling out that he was avoiding me was a calculated move. Calling out the human dynamic honestly, without accusation, often breaks a logjam in a way that formal escalation can't. He responded immediately because I'd made it a conversation between people rather than a bureaucratic exchange.
notes: One of the best influence and political navigation stories

title: EDL Architecture — Reusable Data Bones Vs One-Off Projects
situation: Shortly after transitioning into the AVP, Business Insights & Reporting role, the CDO informed Global Retirement leadership that the new Azure Enterprise Data Lake (EDL) environment was ready to use. Year-end strategic funding was available, and business leaders assembled a list of specific projects: online statement redesign to reduce print-and-post costs (saving ~$20K/year), marketing campaign reporting, and contribution rate analysis. The Deloitte consultants and CDO project team were ready to scope individual deliverables.
obstacle: Each proposed project was a one-off build — a specific report view with hard-coded outputs, no reusability, and no foundational value for the organization beyond its narrow use case. The print-and-post project was the most egregious: the same funding used to build reusable data infrastructure could solve the statements problem and create a platform for everything else. The challenge was that the consultants and CDO team operated in a project-delivery framework — they needed a defined output, user stories, and a cost/benefit case. A proposal to 'build bones' was too abstract for their process and created anxiety about scope creep.
action: Rather than fighting the framework, worked within it. Took on leadership of the contribution rate project — which had a clear, scoped output (average contribution rate by month) — and reframed the build approach internally. Defined the EDL work not as 'build the output report' but as 'gather all the materials in the EDL so that we can model it in Power BI and report the output from there.' This meant building fact and dimension tables at a detailed level, establishing proper unique IDs for cross-table modeling, and designing the architecture for extensibility rather than the minimum required for the immediate deliverable. Delivered on time. Then used Power BI's flexible modeling to generate multi-dimensional analysis — contribution rates by age group, segment, industry, specific client, member, and advisor — within two weeks of the EDL build completing.
result: Presented the approach and results at the CDO Town Hall. Showed live in Power BI how changing the employer match groupings from 0-2%, 2-4% to 1-3%, 3-5% revealed a clear insight: plans that matched at least 3% had significantly higher participant contribution rates, with a sharp inflection at the 3% threshold. Built a targeted list of 500+ clients below the 3% match threshold. Calculated that if only 25 of those clients increased their match by 1%, the financial impact to participants' future retirement health in two years would exceed the project cost by 25x. Received a standing ovation at the Town Hall — the first time anyone had demonstrated genuine insight value from the EDL. The reusable architecture became the standard approach for all future EDL development.
impact: Reusable EDL architecture established as standard; standing ovation at CDO Town Hall; 500+ client opportunity list generated; 25x ROI identified
fullStory: The tension at the center of this story is one I've faced at every organization: the difference between building something that solves one problem and building something that can solve many. The print-and-post project was the extreme example — $20K in annual savings from a build that would have no reuse and no extensibility. I would have used the same funding and the same time to build the underlying data model that made the statements project possible and left us with an asset that could do the contribution rate analysis and fifteen other things we hadn't thought of yet. The room wasn't going to accept the abstract argument. So I didn't make it. I took on the contribution rate project with a specific scope, delivered it exactly as promised, and then showed everyone what you could do once the ingredients were in the right place. The 3% employer match insight was genuinely significant — a clear, actionable finding that nobody had ever surfaced before. The standing ovation at the Town Hall wasn't for the data work. It was for showing people what data work was actually capable of.
notes: EDL story with standing ovation — strong executive influence and data strategy story

title: Participant Outcomes Index — Measuring Retirement Readiness
situation: The retirement business was measuring its success using activity-based metrics: assets under management, sales volume, lapse rates. None of these metrics answered the question that mattered most: were participants actually on track for retirement success? A member with a large balance who was making poor allocation decisions or insufficient contributions might look like a good outcome on an AUM metric while being headed for a retirement shortfall.
obstacle: Building a retirement readiness index requires integrating behavioral, financial, and demographic data in a way that produces a defensible score — one that correlates with actual retirement outcomes, can be calculated consistently across millions of participants, and is explainable to executives, regulators, and eventually plan sponsors and members. The analytical and governance challenges were significant. Defining 'retirement readiness' in a way that was both meaningful and measurable required working through genuine philosophical disagreements about what the business was there to do.
action: Co-led development of the Participant Outcomes Index with the strategy and product teams. Designed a holistic scoring methodology that integrated contribution rate adequacy, asset allocation appropriateness for age/risk profile, engagement level, and projected income replacement ratio. Grounded the methodology in academic research on retirement adequacy. Built the data infrastructure to calculate the index consistently across the participant base. Created dashboards and insight narratives presenting the distribution of retirement readiness scores across segments. Piloted internal rollout, then developed a plan for external rollout into plan sponsor reporting and website integration.
result: The Participant Outcomes Index was adopted as a core KPI at the executive and board level — recognized with the Ovation Award. Launched internally and began rollout into plan sponsor reporting. Generated thought leadership insights on retirement readiness that positioned Manulife as a genuine advocate for participant outcomes, not just an asset accumulator.
impact: Participant Outcomes Index adopted at ELT level; Ovation Award 2025; plan sponsor reporting integration underway
fullStory: The Participant Outcomes Index was the most philosophically ambitious project I worked on, because it required the organization to commit to an answer to a question it had been avoiding: what is the business actually here to do? If the answer is 'accumulate assets,' then AUM is your metric. If the answer is 'help participants retire with financial security,' then you need a different scorecard. Building the index forced that conversation, and it was a better conversation than most organizations have. The methodology work was hard — there's no universally agreed definition of retirement readiness, and the line between 'on track' and 'at risk' involves judgment calls. We worked through those judgment calls with academic rigor and documented every assumption. The internal launch was the exciting part: for the first time, we could see the distribution of retirement health across the participant base by segment, by employer, by region. The insights were sometimes uncomfortable. That was exactly the point.
notes: Ovation Award 2025 — flagship insight innovation story

title: Capture Rate Redesign — Fixing a Structurally Misleading Metric
situation: The capture rate was the primary metric for measuring participant rollover success — the percentage of terminating member assets that rolled over into a Manulife product rather than leaving the plan. On the surface it appeared to be a clean, simple metric. In practice it was incentivizing sales behavior that was wrong for the business: because smaller termination amounts were easier to capture, teams were optimizing for volume of smaller rollovers rather than focusing on the high-AUM terminations that materially affected the business.
obstacle: Leaders were deeply attached to the capture rate. It had been the primary metric for years, it featured prominently in executive reporting, and many leaders had built their strategies around it. Changing it meant not just proposing a better metric but convincing leadership that their current metric was actively misleading them — a significant political challenge. It also required restating historical performance, which meant some periods that had looked strong would look less impressive under the new framework.
action: Deconstructed the existing capture rate metric to document its structural flaws — specifically, the selection bias toward small amounts and the lack of AUM weighting. Built a redesigned capture rate that was AUM-weighted, calculated on a net basis, and segmented to allow comparison across plan size tiers. Created before-and-after examples showing how the same historical period told a different story under the old and new metric. Socialized the change with global CFOs and regional leaders, starting with the analytical case before any governance discussion. Aligned global leadership on the new definition before formalizing it in reporting.
result: Leadership adopted the redesigned capture rate metric globally. Decision-making quality improved immediately — the team was now optimizing for the right outcomes. The before-and-after comparison was cited by the CFO as one of the most clear examples of insight work changing the direction of the business.
impact: Capture rate metric redesigned globally; decision-making immediately improved; cited by CFO as high-impact insight work
fullStory: The capture rate story is a microcosm of a broader insight leadership principle: a metric that looks right but measures the wrong thing is worse than having no metric at all, because it creates confidence that you understand something you don't. When I started deconstructing why the capture rate results were so volatile quarter-to-quarter, the answer was obvious: we were capturing small amounts at high rates and large amounts at mediocre rates, and the total metric was being dominated by the small-amount activity. A great quarter wasn't necessarily a great business development quarter — it might just be a quarter with a lot of small terminations. The redesign required restating history, which was uncomfortable. But I've found that executives generally prefer uncomfortable truth to comfortable fiction, especially when you show them the specific decisions that comfortable fiction had caused them to make incorrectly.

title: Data Quality Initiative — Restoring Trust in Enterprise Data
situation: After a series of reporting errors that had surfaced in executive reviews, leadership confidence in the data underpinning decision-making had dropped to a low point. The errors weren't catastrophic individually, but their cumulative effect was damaging — leaders had started adding caveats to data-driven recommendations, and some were reverting to intuition-based decisions because they didn't trust the numbers.
obstacle: Data quality issues are systemic — they live upstream of any single report and often require changes in systems, processes, and team behaviors that the insights function doesn't directly control. Fixing them requires investigation across multiple system owners, data engineers, and business stakeholders, each of whom tends to believe the problem is someone else's responsibility. The governance challenge was as significant as the technical one.
action: Led a root-cause analysis across the key reporting workflows, tracing each identified error back to its source. Built a data quality scorecard that tracked accuracy, completeness, and timeliness metrics by data source. Implemented automated validation checks at the ingestion points for critical data sources so errors were caught before they reached reporting. Established a data quality council with representation from engineering, analytics, and business stakeholders. Partnered with data engineering to fix the upstream issues that were generating the most significant errors.
result: Significant reduction in data errors across critical reporting workflows. Audit readiness improved materially. Trust in the data returned to a level where leaders were no longer adding caveats to data-driven recommendations. The data quality scorecard was adopted as an ongoing governance tool.
impact: Data errors reduced significantly; audit readiness improved; executive trust in data restored
fullStory: The trust problem was the real problem — the errors were almost secondary. When a leader starts saying 'I think the number is approximately right' rather than acting on it, your analytics function has been effectively disabled. So I attacked the trust problem directly: I was transparent about what we'd found in the root-cause analysis, which errors had been most significant, and what we were doing to prevent each one. Transparency about imperfection paradoxically builds more trust than pretending nothing had gone wrong. The automated validation layer was the infrastructure fix — catching errors before they reached reports rather than relying on humans to spot them in review. The data quality council was the governance fix — making data quality everyone's responsibility rather than nobody's. Neither of those changes was glamorous, but both were necessary.

title: Reporting Automation — Freeing Analysts for High-Value Work
situation: A significant portion of the analytics team's capacity was being consumed by manual reporting processes — pulling data, reformatting it, running calculations, and populating templates. This was happening every week across multiple report types, creating a situation where skilled analysts were spending the majority of their time on work that added no insight value and could theoretically be automated.
obstacle: Automation initiatives in analytics teams often fail because teams are skeptical — previous attempts had broken things, or 'automated' reports had turned out to require almost as much manual checking as the original. There was also genuine complexity: many of the reports had undocumented logic that only existed in the memory of the analyst who had always done them.
action: Mapped every manual reporting workflow to document the actual process steps, data sources, and transformation logic. Prioritized automation candidates by a combination of time consumption and automation feasibility. Partnered with the data engineering team to build automated pipelines for the highest-volume reports. Built validation and exception-reporting logic into every automated workflow so that anomalies were flagged rather than silently passed through. Redesigned report templates to support automated data flows. Ran the manual and automated versions in parallel until stability was confirmed.
result: Reporting time reduced dramatically across the highest-volume workflows. Analyst capacity freed for insight generation. Report accuracy improved because the automated process was more consistent than the manual one. The team's identity began to shift as the manual work that had defined the job disappeared.
impact: Manual reporting time dramatically reduced; analyst capacity freed for insight and advisory work
fullStory: The resistance to automation on reporting teams is always the same: 'what if the automated version is wrong?' Which is an interesting objection when you consider that the manual version is wrong regularly, just in ways that are harder to track. I made the case for automation by measuring the error rate on manual reporting first — it was higher than anyone had acknowledged. Then the exception-reporting logic built into every automated workflow became the answer: automated reports don't just run silently, they flag anything unusual and ask a human to review it. That's actually more rigorous than most manual processes, which rely on someone noticing an anomaly as they paste data from one spreadsheet to another. The parallel-running period was critical for building trust. Once the automated and manual outputs had matched for eight consecutive weeks, the resistance largely disappeared.

title: AI-Enabled Insight Workflows — Embedding AI into Daily Operations
situation: AI was present in the organization primarily as a project — discussed in strategy meetings, explored in proof-of-concepts, but not embedded in how the analytics and insight teams actually worked day-to-day. The risk was that AI remained a technology curiosity rather than a productivity and quality multiplier.
obstacle: Teams lacked both confidence and practical models for how to integrate AI tools into analytical work. There were also governance concerns about AI-generated content in regulated outputs. The cultural challenge was significant: analytics professionals often have a 'we do this ourselves' identity that can create resistance to tools that feel like they're doing their job.
action: Championed AI adoption as a daily work tool, not a project. Built AI-enabled workflows for hypothesis generation, narrative drafting, pattern interpretation, and research synthesis. Integrated AI prompting into the insight review process — using it to pressure-test analytical conclusions and surface alternative interpretations. Co-led the integration of Microsoft Copilot into Power BI dashboards, enabling natural-language querying of data. Trained teams on prompt engineering, hypothesis-driven analysis with AI, and the appropriate limits of AI-generated output in regulated contexts. Built a culture where using AI was expected rather than exceptional.
result: Analysis cycle time reduced materially. Quality of strategic output improved as AI was used to test assumptions and generate alternative framings that human analysts might not have considered. The team built genuine AI fluency — practical skill in getting high-quality output from AI tools, not just familiarity with the concept.
impact: AI embedded in daily analytical workflows; Copilot integrated into enterprise dashboards; analysis cycle time materially reduced
fullStory: The thing about AI adoption in analytics is that the resistance isn't usually technological — it's identity. Analysts have spent careers developing expertise in doing things manually, and a tool that does those things faster feels threatening rather than freeing. I reframed it deliberately: AI isn't replacing the analytical work, it's replacing the parts of the analytical work that weren't really analytical — the data pulling, the narrative first draft, the literature scan. What's left is the part that requires judgment, context, and expertise that the AI doesn't have. The Copilot integration in Power BI was the most visible win: business users who had always needed to make a data request could now just ask a question in plain language. That changed the dynamic between the insights function and the business — from 'request an analysis and wait' to 'get an answer in thirty seconds and come back if you need something deeper.'

title: Behavioural Segmentation — Understanding Member Personas
situation: The business was treating its 5 million participants as a relatively homogeneous group — same communications, same product offers, same service model — with modest variation by plan size and age. This approach was efficient but was leaving significant value on the table: members with very different financial behaviors and needs were receiving the same interventions, and the interventions were optimized for an average member who didn't really exist.
obstacle: Building a meaningful segmentation model required integrating behavioral, demographic, and plan-level data across systems that had never been joined at the member level. Previous segmentation work had been done statistically on single data sources rather than across the integrated picture of a member's actual behavior. There was also a business skepticism: would segmentation actually change anything, or would it just add analytical complexity without changing the services members received?
action: Consolidated behavioral, demographic, and plan-level data into a unified member-level model. Applied clustering analysis to identify behavioral cohorts based on contribution patterns, engagement behaviors, product usage, and financial outcome indicators. Validated the cohorts against outcome data — retirement readiness, lapse rates, rollover conversion — to confirm that the segments predicted meaningfully different behaviors. Built persona playbooks for each segment describing the member's typical situation, the interventions most likely to improve their outcomes, and the communication style most likely to resonate. Presented to product, marketing, and service teams with clear strategic implications.
result: The segmentation framework was adopted as the analytical foundation for product development, marketing strategy, and service design. Targeting precision improved across all three functions. The persona playbooks became living documents that evolved as the underlying behavioral data updated.
impact: Behavioral segmentation adopted across product, marketing, and service; targeting precision improved
fullStory: The segmentation work was one of those projects where the hardest part was making it actionable rather than just interesting. Building clusters is not hard analytically. Getting those clusters to change how three separate business functions operate is the challenge. I built the persona playbooks specifically to bridge that gap: rather than presenting a statistical report that said 'here are five clusters with these characteristics,' I translated each cluster into a story — this is who this member is, this is what they're likely to be worried about, this is what they respond to. That narrative translation was what made the segmentation usable by people who weren't data scientists. The service team could think about a member in terms of their persona and adapt their approach. The marketing team could build content that spoke to a specific situation rather than a statistical average.

title: Addressable Market Dashboard — Market Intelligence Engine
situation: The distribution organization had limited visibility into the total addressable market and Manulife's position within it. They could see internal results — what they sold, to whom, through which advisors — but had no systematic view of competitive positioning, market share by segment, or where the largest white-space opportunities existed relative to industry peers.
obstacle: Integrating external industry data with internal sales, retention, and advisor relationship data required both technical work and vendor management. External datasets were expensive, inconsistently structured, and required significant processing to become analytically useful alongside internal data. There was also a question of analytical validity — market share calculations using external data require assumptions about coverage and comparability that needed to be documented and defensible.
action: Sourced and integrated purchased industry datasets with internal AIR data. Built a segmentation model that divided the market by plan size, advisor relationship type, industry, and region. Created dashboards showing Manulife's position in each segment — share of wallet with existing advisors, win rates by advisor type, white-space maps showing high-opportunity areas with low current coverage. Introduced the national accounts model using a 6-box framework that segmented advisors by total book size and their share of business with Manulife — enabling targeted strategies for large advisors with high market share elsewhere but low Manulife penetration.
result: Distribution leadership gained a systematic view of competitive positioning for the first time. The national accounts model reshaped how the sales organization thought about advisor investment — moving from coverage-based resource allocation to share-of-wallet-based prioritization. Specific high-opportunity segments and advisors were identified and targeted.
impact: National accounts model adopted; distribution strategy shifted to share-of-wallet prioritization; market white-space mapped
fullStory: The 6-box national accounts model was the most commercially impactful piece of the Addressable Market Dashboard. The insight it captured was simple: the advisors who sell Manulife almost exclusively but have small total books are not the same strategic priority as advisors who have enormous total books but barely interact with Manulife. Both might look the same in an internal sales report — they might even have the same Manulife revenue. But their opportunity profiles are completely different. The 6-box let the distribution team see which advisors deserved deep investment — the ones with large total books and low Manulife penetration — and which were already maximized. That changed how we allocated relationship management resources more than any other single piece of analysis I can remember.

title: Digital Engagement Metrics — Measuring Digital Leadership
situation: The Retirement business was investing significantly in digital capabilities — app enhancements, portal improvements, digital advice tools — but had no consistent measurement framework for evaluating whether these investments were changing member behavior. Different teams used different definitions for 'digital engagement,' making cross-initiative comparison impossible and investment prioritization difficult.
obstacle: Digital engagement is inherently multi-dimensional — frequency, depth, type of action, impact on downstream behavior all matter, and no single metric captures it. Teams had gravitated toward the metrics that made their initiatives look best rather than toward a set of metrics that told the genuine story of digital leadership. Getting alignment required both an analytical argument and a governance conversation.
action: Defined a hierarchical digital engagement framework: tier one metrics for executive-level tracking (active digital users, digital penetration rate, digital actions per active user); tier two metrics for product-level evaluation (feature adoption rates, session depth, digital advice engagement). Built the measurement infrastructure in GRID to calculate these consistently. Socialized the framework with product, digital, and strategy teams. Integrated the tier one metrics into executive reporting.
result: ELT adopted the digital engagement metrics as standard KPIs, replacing the fragmented approach. Product teams had a shared language for evaluating digital investment performance. Digital strategy conversations became more analytical and less anecdotal.
impact: Digital engagement metrics adopted at ELT level; consistent measurement framework enabled investment prioritization
fullStory: The digital metrics problem is a classic governance problem wearing an analytics costume. Every team was measuring digital engagement — it was just that nobody was measuring the same thing. I spent the first two weeks just mapping what existed: seventeen different 'active user' definitions across six teams. The analytical work of building a good framework was straightforward. The governance work of getting everyone to abandon their existing definitions in favor of a shared one was the actual challenge. I handled it by being transparent about the business cost of the current state: when the CFO asks 'are we winning digitally?' and the answer depends on which team you ask, that's a problem. Once I made the case in those terms, the resistance largely melted — nobody wanted to be the team defending a definition that existed primarily because it made their numbers look better.

title: Daily NAV Platform — First-of-Its-Kind Property Fund Operation
situation: Manulife Private Asset Management needed to offer a property-based fund with daily liquidity — a product structure that had never been built in Canada at this scale. Real estate funds traditionally priced monthly or quarterly because property valuations don't move daily the way market securities do. Building daily NAV capability for a real estate fund required solving a genuinely novel operational problem.
obstacle: The data required for daily NAV — property-level financials from YARDI, transfer agency transaction records, third-party valuations, and debt management positions — lived in completely separate systems with no existing integration. Building the integration required partnering with CITCO (the external fund administrator), designing reconciliation workflows that could catch errors before they reached the NAV calculation, and ensuring compliance with fund accounting standards for a new product type.
action: Partnered with CITCO to design the daily NAV architecture from the ground up. Mapped each data source — YARDI property data, transfer agency records, third-party valuation files, debt management system — and designed the integration and transformation logic to bring them together into a daily NAV calculation. Built reconciliation and validation workflows at each integration point. Designed the reporting infrastructure to support client reporting, financial statements, and tax filing requirements from the same underlying data model. Ensured the architecture met fund accounting standards and audit requirements for the new product type.
result: Successfully launched daily NAV capability for the property fund — to my knowledge the first Canadian retirement fund with exposure to direct real properties that could be traded daily. The product structure enabled a new class of fund design that had not previously been possible in the market. Recognized with the Pinnacle Award for Service Excellence.
impact: First daily NAV property fund in Canada; Pinnacle Award for Service Excellence; enabled new product structures
fullStory: The daily NAV problem for a property fund is interesting because it sounds impossible at first. Properties don't get marked to market daily. How do you calculate a NAV? The answer is that you build a model that separates what changes daily — cash flows, transaction volumes, debt positions, transfer agency activity — from what doesn't. The property valuations update periodically, but the model handles them correctly at each update point. The CITCO partnership was critical: they had operational infrastructure we needed and we had analytical architecture design expertise they didn't. The daily NAV wasn't just a technical achievement — it enabled a product that investors actually wanted: property exposure with the liquidity they were accustomed to from market securities. That combination had never been available to Canadian retirement plans before. When I received the Pinnacle Award I thought the more important recognition was the client base that was able to access this product for the first time.
notes: Pinnacle Award. First daily NAV property fund in Canada.

title: LTT Ruling Reversal — Protecting 1% Annual Client Returns
situation: The Ontario Ministry of Finance retroactively removed a de minimis exemption for Land Transfer Tax on property transactions within a fund-of-funds structure — a change that would have resulted in triple taxation of the same property transaction. Deloitte, as external advisor, had reviewed the ruling and concluded it must be accepted. The financial impact was approximately 1% in annual client returns.
obstacle: Challenging a major accounting firm's legal interpretation of a tax ruling, with a government ministry involved, is not a comfortable position. The organizational instinct was to accept Deloitte's conclusion and move on. Pushing back required independent technical analysis to identify where Deloitte's interpretation was flawed, and then direct engagement with both Deloitte and the Ministry — which required navigating carefully to avoid damaging the relationship with Deloitte while still pressing the case.
action: Conducted an independent technical review of the LTT ruling and Deloitte's interpretation. Identified the specific flaw in the analysis: the application of the de minimis threshold to the fund-of-funds structure was being calculated incorrectly, not accounting for the disaggregation of beneficial ownership across the fund's participants. Prepared a detailed technical counter-analysis. Engaged directly with Deloitte to present the alternative interpretation, and subsequently arranged for direct dialogue with Ministry of Finance representatives. Advocated consistently for the correct interpretation throughout.
result: The Ministry of Finance issued a revised ruling accepting the alternative interpretation. Triple taxation was avoided. Approximately 1% of annual client returns was protected. The Manulife private markets platform's credibility as a sophisticated technical operator was strengthened.
impact: ~1% annual client returns protected; Ontario LTT triple-taxation avoided; Pinnacle Award for Service Excellence
fullStory: The LTT ruling story is about the willingness to push back on expert consensus when you've done the analysis to know it's wrong. Deloitte had concluded the ruling had to be accepted. They're Deloitte — there's a significant organizational gravitational pull toward accepting their conclusion and moving on. But when I reviewed the analysis, I could see where the error was: the calculation of the de minimis threshold wasn't accounting properly for the fund-of-funds structure. That's a technical point but it's a consequential one — a 1% return impact on a multi-billion-dollar private markets portfolio is real money for real clients. The Ministry conversation was delicate. We weren't going in adversarially — we were going in with a specific technical argument about the correct application of the ruling to our structure. They reviewed it and agreed. That outcome — a government ministry issuing a revised ruling — is not a common one, and it required both the analytical rigor to build an airtight case and the willingness to have the uncomfortable conversation.
notes: Pinnacle Award. One of the most distinctive regulatory advocacy stories.

title: Building Private Markets Operations — From Zero to Scalable Function
situation: Manulife was launching and scaling a private markets business — real estate and private asset portfolios — that needed operational, analytical, and governance infrastructure built from inception. There was no existing blueprint, no team, no data infrastructure, and no vendor relationships. The business was growing rapidly and needed the operational backbone to support it.
obstacle: Building from zero while the business is actively transacting is one of the hardest operational challenges — you're designing the factory floor while the factory is running. Every new deal created new operational requirements. The choice of third-party administrator (ultimately CITCO and State Street) had to be made early and got locked in, making vendor selection critical. The 'gold-copy book of record' — determining which system's data was authoritative when multiple systems disagreed — was both technically and politically complex.
action: Designed the full operating framework for private markets operations: team structure, data architecture, governance model, and vendor oversight model. Hired key roles and built the team. Selected and onboarded CITCO as the primary fund administrator, establishing SLAs, reporting standards, and oversight processes. Built the gold-copy book of record — the definitive logic for resolving data conflicts between YARDI, transfer agency systems, valuations, and the fund administrator. Established reporting, audit, and governance standards. Created the oversight model for third-party relationships.
result: The operations function became the foundational infrastructure for private markets growth. Long-standing data inconsistencies were eliminated through the gold-copy framework. The governance model withstood multiple regulatory examinations. The function scaled successfully as the portfolio grew.
impact: Private markets operations built from inception; gold-copy data framework established; function scaled with portfolio growth; Pinnacle Award for Service Excellence
fullStory: Building something from nothing is the purest test of operational design. There's no existing process to inherit or fix — every choice you make is deliberate. The gold-copy book of record was the most consequential design decision: when YARDI, the transfer agent, the valuation provider, and the fund administrator all have a different number, which one is right? The answer has to be a rule, not a conversation — because when it's a conversation it's a different conversation every time, and different conversations produce different answers. I built the priority hierarchy explicitly: for NAV-affecting positions, the fund administrator's reconciled position is authoritative; for property-level data, YARDI is authoritative unless overridden by a formal valuation. That rule, documented and enforced, eliminated probably 80% of the data disputes that would otherwise have happened.
notes: Pinnacle Award

title: Fund Conversions — High-Risk, Zero-Error Delivery at State Street
situation: State Street was onboarding several large institutional fund clients requiring complex conversions — data migrations, portfolio reconciliations, operational redesigns, and system transitions — all with tight client-mandated timelines and zero tolerance for error. A conversion error that affected a client's NAV, reporting, or compliance position could damage the relationship and expose State Street to financial liability.
obstacle: Fund conversions involve coordinating multiple teams — fund accounting, client services, technology, custody, and the client's own operations team — all with different priorities and limited familiarity with each other's processes. The client's legacy data was often inconsistently structured, requiring significant investigation before migration could proceed. The timeline pressure meant there was minimal room to discover problems late.
action: Led cross-functional teams through end-to-end conversion cycles, establishing a structured project methodology: comprehensive documentation of the client's legacy data structure, future-state workflow design, explicit risk identification and mitigation planning, checkpoint-based validation, and rehearsal runs before the live conversion date. Mapped legacy processes and designed future-state workflows for each client. Built detailed controls and ran simulated failure scenarios to identify points of vulnerability. Maintained direct communication with client leadership throughout.
result: Delivered multiple major fund conversions on time with zero critical issues across all clients handled. Client relationships were strengthened through the professionalism of the conversion process. Internal processes improved as learnings were incorporated into the conversion methodology.
impact: Multiple major fund conversions delivered with zero critical issues; client relationships strengthened
fullStory: Fund conversions taught me more about project risk management than anything else in my career. The fundamental challenge is that you can't fully understand the complexity of what you're converting until you're deep into the process — and by then you've committed to a timeline. The answer is to front-load your risk identification: spend more time than feels comfortable in the pre-work phase documenting exactly what you're dealing with and simulating what can go wrong. The rehearsal runs were non-negotiable for me. I would run the full conversion sequence on copied data before the live date, and I would deliberately inject the most likely error types to see how the team responded. The clients never saw the chaos that sometimes happened in rehearsal — they only saw a clean live execution. That's the whole point.

title: ETF Launches — Operationalizing BlackRock & Vanguard's First Canadian ETFs
situation: Both BlackRock and Vanguard were entering the Canadian ETF market and had selected State Street as their operational partner. These were landmark capital markets product launches — the first Canadian ETFs for two of the world's largest asset managers — and they required building NAV processes, reporting infrastructure, and compliance workflows for a product structure that was relatively new to the Canadian market.
obstacle: ETF operational structure requires specialized NAV processes — in-kind creation and redemption baskets, intraday pricing management, authorized participant workflows — that are distinct from traditional fund accounting. The existing State Street infrastructure was built for traditional mutual funds and pooled accounts, not for the specific mechanics of ETF creation/redemption. Building for two landmark client relationships simultaneously meant there was no margin for learning failures.
action: Partnered with the client solutions teams from BlackRock and Vanguard to understand the operational requirements of their specific ETF structures. Designed the NAV production workflow, including the in-kind basket calculation process, authorized participant settlement, and intraday pricing feeds. Built the reporting and reconciliation processes. Coordinated with compliance and legal to ensure the workflows met regulatory and audit requirements for the new product type.
result: Both ETFs launched successfully and on time. State Street was established as a credible operational partner for ETF products in the Canadian market. The operational infrastructure built for these launches became the template for subsequent ETF client relationships.
impact: BlackRock and Vanguard's first Canadian ETFs launched successfully; State Street's ETF operational capability established
fullStory: Being part of the team that launched BlackRock and Vanguard's first Canadian ETFs is one of those career moments you don't fully appreciate until later. At the time it was just an intensely technical, high-pressure project. ETF mechanics are genuinely different from traditional fund accounting — the in-kind creation and redemption process, the authorized participant workflow, the intraday pricing — these require completely different operational logic. The fact that we were doing it for two of the world's largest asset managers simultaneously, and that these were landmark firsts for the Canadian market, meant the scrutiny was at a level I hadn't experienced before. The launches went cleanly. The real achievement wasn't the launch day — it was the six months of design and process-building that made launch day boring.
notes: BlackRock and Vanguard's first Canadian ETFs

title: Offshoring Transformation — Sustaining Quality Through Change
situation: State Street undertook a significant offshoring initiative, transitioning portions of fund accounting operations from Canada to India. I was leading the Canadian fund accounting team of 100+ staff during this transition — responsible for maintaining service quality and client satisfaction while managing the significant human and operational challenges that come with large-scale workforce change.
obstacle: The risk profile of offshoring fund operations is high: client SLAs don't change, regulatory requirements don't change, but the team doing the work is being rebuilt from the ground up in a different geography, culture, and time zone. Team morale in Canada was fragile — staff were uncertain about their own roles and some were actively disengaged. The offshore team was building capability from zero under real-time production pressure.
action: Simplified processes before migrating them — removing complexity that had accumulated over years and that would have made offshore training unnecessarily difficult. Built structured training and oversight models for the offshore team, ensuring knowledge transfer was documented rather than relying on verbal instruction. Maintained strong, proactive communication with clients throughout the transition — not waiting for problems to surface but giving clients advance visibility into what was changing and confidence in the quality controls. Coached the Canadian management team on navigating the change personally and professionally while maintaining performance.
result: Service quality was sustained through the transition — no material client incidents attributable to the offshoring. Client satisfaction was maintained. The offshore team built capability faster than projected. The Canadian team retained its highest performers.
impact: Service quality sustained; client satisfaction maintained; no material incidents through offshoring transition
fullStory: Offshoring transitions are fundamentally people problems that often get treated as logistics problems. The logistics — what gets moved when, how the handoffs work, what the escalation paths are — those are solvable. The harder problem is that you're asking people in Canada to teach their jobs to a team in India, knowing that their own role may be reduced or eliminated. That's not a comfortable dynamic. I handled it by being as honest as I could about what was happening, what roles were affected, and what the organization was doing to support people through it. I also focused intensely on who we were keeping — making sure the best people felt valued and had a reason to stay. The simplification-before-migration principle was the most operationally important decision I made. We identified about forty process steps across the fund accounting workflows that had been built on legacy workarounds and were unnecessarily complex. Simplifying them before migrating made the offshore training dramatically easier and also reduced the error rate in Canada in the meantime.

title: Third-Party Governance — Managing Citco and State Street Oversight
situation: Manulife Private Markets relied on external fund administrators — primarily CITCO — for fund accounting, NAV production, and client reporting. As the business scaled, the informal oversight model that had been sufficient at inception was creating risk: errors were being caught late, reporting was inconsistent, and there was no systematic process for escalation when deliverables were missed.
obstacle: Managing a vendor who is also a critical operational partner requires a different approach than managing a simple service provider. CITCO was doing complex, technically challenging work on tight timelines — an adversarial relationship would have been counterproductive. The governance model needed to create accountability without damaging the collaborative relationship that made the partnership work.
action: Designed a formal oversight framework for the CITCO relationship: SLAs with explicit definitions of on-time delivery and accuracy standards, a tiered escalation path for performance issues, regular structured performance reviews, and reconciliation and validation processes that caught errors before they affected client deliverables. Built internal oversight capabilities — not to duplicate CITCO's work but to validate it at key checkpoints. Ensured alignment between CITCO's reporting outputs and Manulife's internal reporting standards.
result: Reporting accuracy and timeliness improved materially. The escalation framework meant problems were caught and resolved faster. The relationship with CITCO remained collaborative because the governance model was transparent and consistently applied rather than reactive and punitive.
impact: Fund administrator performance materially improved; reporting accuracy and timeliness enhanced; governance model adopted as standard
fullStory: The CITCO governance story is about the right design of vendor oversight in high-stakes operational partnerships. CITCO was not a bad vendor — they were doing technically complex work. The problem was the absence of a shared framework for how we'd know if they were doing it well. Without explicit SLAs, every conversation about a performance issue became a negotiation about whether it was actually a problem. With SLAs, the question was just factual: was the NAV delivered by 5pm? Was the reconciliation variance within the agreed tolerance? That clarity was actually better for the relationship because it removed ambiguity about expectations. The governance framework I built wasn't designed to catch CITCO out — it was designed to give both parties the same clear picture of how we were doing.

title: Turning Objectors Into Champions — Cultural Transformation
situation: The analysts on my team were skilled at reporting — collecting data, building tables, distributing reports on schedule. They were not skilled at or comfortable with insight work — forming hypotheses, testing them, writing narrative, presenting conclusions, engaging with executives about what the data meant. The culture was deeply entrenched in the belief that interpretation was someone else's job.
obstacle: The resistance wasn't laziness — it was a combination of fear and identity. Analysts feared that having an opinion meant being wrong, and being wrong in front of a senior leader was something their entire career had trained them to avoid. Their identity was built around accuracy and timeliness, not judgment. Asking them to start offering interpretation felt like a threat to the professional identity they'd spent years building.
action: Built psychological safety through deliberate coaching. Started by modeling the behavior myself — bringing in my own analytical interpretations and being explicit when I was uncertain, showing that having an opinion and being wrong occasionally was survivable and valued. Created insight templates that gave analysts a structure for framing their thinking without having to start from a blank page. Ran weekly insight reviews where teams presented their interpretations in a supportive context before presenting to leaders. Celebrated early wins publicly and explicitly. Addressed the fear of being wrong directly: 'Your commentary is a hypothesis, not a verdict. The leader might disagree, and that's a conversation, not a failure.' Automated the manual reporting work to create the time for this new way of working.
result: Analysts transformed. Within six months, the team was proactively surfacing insights in executive conversations, not waiting to be asked. Leaders were forwarding insight summaries to other executives. The team earned the Stars of Excellence Award for the transformation. Several analysts were promoted as a result of capabilities they hadn't had before.
impact: Team culture transformed; analysts became strategic advisors; Stars of Excellence Award; multiple analyst promotions
fullStory: The transformation started with one analyst — Maya — who had been with the team for years and was meticulous, reliable, and completely unwilling to offer an opinion. After three months of coaching, she presented an insight in an executive review that wasn't just technically accurate — it was genuinely analytical. She'd identified a counterintuitive pattern in member contribution data and was willing to say out loud what she thought it meant. The CFO pushed back. Maya didn't fold. She said, 'I could be wrong, but here's my reasoning.' The CFO said, 'That's actually a good way to look at it.' That moment changed the team's culture more than anything I did directly. When other analysts saw that having an opinion in front of a senior leader wasn't career-ending — that it might actually be career-building — the transformation accelerated. That's how culture change actually works: one visible moment at a time.
notes: Stars of Excellence Award

title: Leading Through Resistance — Breaking a 6-Month Impasse in 48 Hours
situation: A critical client-facing report — a Plan Design Review tool used by distribution RMs with plan sponsors — had stopped working due to a data access conflict between the Advanced Analytics team and the CDO organization. Tom, one of my directors, had been working to resolve it for six months through the normal channels: sending requests, involving the data teams, proposing workarounds. None of them worked. The problem had calcified into a political standoff, with both teams believing the other was responsible and neither willing to take the risk of resolving it unilaterally.
obstacle: The actual fix was not technically complex — it required someone with sufficient authority to grant explicit permission for the data access that was being blocked by the CDO's new PROD vs non-PROD rules. The problem was that nobody had identified who that person was, and the person who needed to act (Ben, the Advanced Analytics data lead) was avoiding all conversations about it because his team's compliance risk was real. Six months of progress had produced six months of workarounds that didn't work.
action: Identified the likely root cause through a casual conversation with the CDO VP — the PROD vs non-PROD rule change and its unintended impact on Advanced Analytics data. Reached out to Ben directly. He didn't attend the first scheduled meeting. Or the second. Sent him an IM that openly named what was happening: 'It feels like you're avoiding this meeting, and I think I understand why — you're carrying a compliance risk and I haven't given you a way out of it. Let me fix that.' He responded immediately. In the 30-minute meeting that followed, understood his position clearly and asked the one question that unlocked everything: 'Who needs to sign off on this for you to feel comfortable continuing to provide the data while we build a permanent solution?' Within 30 minutes of ending the call, had email authorization from my boss (the COO) and the CDO VP, with Ben and his manager copied. The data access was restored the next day.
result: A 6-month impasse resolved in 48 hours. The client-facing report was restored. The approach — naming the human dynamic openly and reframing the conversation from 'can you help us' to 'let me remove your risk' — was cited by multiple leaders as a model for handling similar situations.
impact: 6-month impasse resolved in 48 hours; client-facing capability restored; model for cross-functional conflict resolution
fullStory: The thing about organizational impasses is that they almost never persist because nobody knows the solution. Usually everyone knows the solution — or at least a path to it. They persist because of incentives: someone is being asked to take a risk for someone else's benefit, and the ask isn't being accompanied by a way to protect them. Ben knew that providing us the data without authorization put his team's compliance standing at risk. From his perspective, every response he gave was either a workaround that bought time or a silence that preserved optionality. The fix was always the same: get the authorization. I just needed to take responsibility for getting it rather than waiting for him to find the courage to ask for it himself. The IM that called out the avoidance was a calculated move. Not aggressive — just honest. Naming the dynamic in a way that showed I understood his position rather than judging it. That's what made the meeting happen. And the meeting was thirty minutes. That's all it took to resolve six months of stalemate.
notes: One of the best political navigation and influence stories

title: High-Engagement Leadership — 90th Percentile Team Score
situation: The Business Insights & Reporting team I inherited had experienced significant turnover, a period of unclear leadership direction, and the accumulated frustration of being seen as a reporting service rather than a strategic function. The annual engagement survey reflected this: the team's score was in the middle of the pack, with specific low ratings on 'I understand how my work contributes to strategy' and 'I have opportunities to grow.'
obstacle: Improving engagement scores is easy to do performatively and hard to do genuinely. The team didn't need team-building events or better perks — they needed to feel that their work mattered and that they had a future in the organization. Both of those things required structural changes, not gestures.
action: Set clear role definitions and expectations so every team member knew what their job was and how it connected to the function's mission. Built individual development plans with each direct report and each analyst — not as a compliance exercise but as genuine conversations about where people wanted to go and what I could do to help them get there. Created psychological safety by modeling vulnerability: being explicit about uncertainty, sharing my own thinking process, encouraging questions without judgment. Removed low-value work through automation so people were spending time on things that developed them rather than things that kept them busy. Celebrated wins publicly and specifically, not generically.
result: Team engagement score reached the 90th percentile — one of the highest in the COO organization. Turnover dropped. Several analysts were promoted. The team's reputation across the business as a strategic partner (rather than a reporting service) improved materially, which in turn created more meaningful work for the team to do.
impact: Team engagement reached 90th percentile; turnover reduced; multiple analyst promotions; team reputation elevated
fullStory: The highest-engagement conversation I had with my team wasn't about engagement at all — it was a conversation with an analyst named Derek who had been thinking about leaving. He felt like his career had stalled. He was doing good work but didn't see a path forward. I spent an hour with him just listening and then being genuinely honest: here's what I see in you, here's where I think you could go, here's what I'll do to help make that happen. He stayed. He was promoted 18 months later. That conversation cost me one hour and it probably retained someone who took years to develop and would have taken years to replace. The 90th percentile score was the aggregate of dozens of conversations like that — conversations where the message was 'you matter, your work matters, and I'm paying attention to you specifically, not just to your outputs.'
notes: 90th percentile engagement score

title: Coaching & Capability Building — Developing Future Leaders
situation: The insights and analytics function had strong technical talent but a significant gap at the strategic advisory level — analysts who could build models and produce reports but who were not yet able to independently scope a problem, design an analytical approach, and communicate conclusions to a senior audience. The pipeline for the advisory capabilities the function needed to build was thin.
obstacle: Developing strategic advisory capability in analytical professionals is slower and harder than developing technical skills, because it requires changing how people think about their role rather than just teaching new tools. It requires a level of coaching investment that is difficult to scale and that many managers deprioritize in favor of delivery.
action: Built a structured development approach: capability frameworks showing what progression from analyst to strategic advisor looked like at each career stage; weekly coaching sessions with each direct report focused on the quality of their thinking rather than the quality of their outputs; narrative feedback on every deliverable — not just 'this is right/wrong' but 'here's how the executive read this, here's what they were looking for, here's how to close the gap.' Created an insight library of strong examples — real analyses and narratives from the team's own work — that analysts could use as models. Encouraged experimentation: analysts were explicitly protected from professional consequences for trying new analytical approaches that didn't work.
result: Multiple analysts moved up the capability curve at a rate that was visible and recognized. Promotions followed. The team's average strategic advisory capability improved measurably as assessed by the quality of outputs and the frequency with which leaders were independently seeking out team members for input.
impact: Multiple analyst promotions; average strategic advisory capability improved; leaders seeking team members independently for input
fullStory: The most important realization I had about developing analytical talent was that the blocker is almost always confidence, not capability. Analysts who've been rewarded for being right are terrified of being wrong in a high-stakes environment. The development work is fundamentally about building the tolerance for uncertainty and the courage to have an opinion. I did that by making it safe: framing feedback as professional development rather than performance evaluation, celebrating interpretations that turned out to be wrong when the reasoning process was sound, and being explicit about the times when I myself had been wrong and what I'd learned. The insight library was a practical tool — it demystified what 'good' looked like. Aspiring to an abstract standard is hard. Aspiring to match a specific example of excellent work that someone you respect produced is much more tractable.

title: Leading Through Ambiguity — Structuring the Unstructured
situation: A recurring characteristic of senior strategy and analytics roles is that the most important problems arrive unstructured — an executive has a concern they can't quite articulate, a business challenge that doesn't fit any existing framework, a political situation that requires analytical input without a clear brief. The ability to take an ambiguous, politically sensitive problem and turn it into a structured, solvable workstream is one of the most valuable and least teachable skills in organizational life.
obstacle: Teams are often afraid to take ownership of ambiguous problems because they're afraid of scoping them incorrectly. The political risk of misdefining the problem can be significant — you spend three months on the wrong question and the executive's disappointment is compounded by the cost of your time. So teams tend to wait for clarity that never comes, or define the problem so narrowly that they're guaranteed to succeed at something that doesn't matter.
action: Developed a consistent approach to ambiguous problem-taking: begin by clarifying the real decision the executive is trying to make before defining the analytical question. Ask: 'At the end of this work, what do you want to be able to decide or do?' Map the stakeholders and their actual interests (not their stated positions). Identify the key tension in the situation — usually between two things the organization wants simultaneously that are in conflict. Structure the workstream around resolving that tension rather than answering the surface question. Present the problem definition back to the executive for validation before beginning the analysis.
result: Consistently delivered clarity in situations where clarity was previously elusive. Executives began bringing ambiguous problems to the function proactively — the reputation for being able to structure the unstructured became a competitive advantage for the team.
impact: Function became the organizational 'go-to' for ambiguous, politically sensitive analytical problems
fullStory: The best articulation I've found for what I do in these situations is that I translate. Executives don't usually know how to ask analytical questions — they know what's bothering them, and they express it in political and strategic terms. My job is to take that expression of concern and translate it into an analytical question that has a defensible answer. The translation step is where most analytical projects go wrong: somebody takes the executive's words literally and answers the literal question, which is almost never the actual question. I once spent two weeks reframing a project that had been running for four months before it was given to me. The team had done good analytical work on the wrong question. The fix was going back to the executive with a different problem statement. She said, 'That's exactly what I was trying to ask.' Four months of work that could have been two weeks if the problem had been structured correctly at the start.

title: Executive Communication — Translating Complexity Into Clarity
situation: A persistent frustration across the analytics and strategy function was the gap between the quality of analytical work and the impact it had in executive conversations. Teams were producing rigorous, well-supported analyses that were being set aside in meetings where the discussion was driven by intuition and political considerations. The work was good; the communication wasn't.
obstacle: Analytical professionals are trained to be comprehensive — to show their work, qualify their conclusions, present alternative interpretations. These are scientific virtues. They are not executive communication virtues. Executives making strategic decisions need a clear recommendation and the three most important supporting points, not a comprehensive methodological overview. Teaching that distinction to people who have built their careers on analytical thoroughness is genuinely difficult.
action: Built executive communication templates that forced analysts to answer three questions before any other consideration: What decision does this analysis inform? What is my recommendation? What are the three things the executive most needs to understand? Ran regular 'narrative reviews' where I would read an analyst's draft as if I were the executive — identifying the places where the analysis got in the way of the message. Coached teams on the SCR structure (Situation-Complication-Resolution) for structuring executive narratives. Presented directly to ELT on a regular basis, modeling the communication approach I was coaching.
result: The quality of executive communication from the function improved measurably. Insight reports began being cited in strategic conversations. Leaders started sharing them proactively with peers. The function's strategic influence increased as a direct consequence of the communication improvement.
impact: Executive communication quality improved; insight reports cited and shared proactively by senior leaders; function's strategic influence increased
fullStory: The most common mistake I see analytical professionals make in executive communication is trying to prove that they're right rather than helping the executive make a decision. Those are different jobs. When you're trying to prove you're right, you lead with evidence and build to a conclusion. When you're helping someone decide, you lead with the recommendation and use evidence to build confidence in it. The shift sounds small but it changes everything — where you spend your time in the document, what you put on slide one, how you handle pushback. I've given the same underlying analysis in both formats and seen completely different reactions from the same executive. The version that led with the recommendation got acted on. The version that led with the methodology got a 'very interesting, send me the deck' response that was politely never followed up.

title: Building Psychological Safety — Unlocking Team Performance
situation: When I stepped into leadership of the Business Insights & Reporting team, the culture had some indicators of low psychological safety: analysts were reluctant to share ideas they weren't sure about, questions were framed as statements to avoid the appearance of not knowing, and there was visible anxiety around senior leader interactions. These patterns were suppressing the creative, hypothesis-driven work that I needed the team to do.
obstacle: Building psychological safety requires consistent leadership behavior over a sustained period — it can't be created by a single speech or team event. It also requires specific changes in how feedback, failure, and uncertainty are treated, which means changing habits that leaders and team members have built over careers. In an organization that has historically rewarded accuracy and certainty, creating space for questions and experimentation requires going against the prevailing culture.
action: Modeled the behavior I wanted explicitly: in team settings, acknowledged my own uncertainty, shared my reasoning process including the points where I wasn't sure, and asked questions I didn't know the answer to. Changed how I gave feedback: separated 'what I observed' from 'my interpretation' from 'my recommendation' — making my own reasoning transparent and fallible rather than authoritative. When analysts tried something that didn't work, led the debrief by asking 'what would you do differently' rather than 'what went wrong.' Explicitly normalized questions by thanking people publicly for asking them: 'That's exactly the right question to be asking.'
result: Observable shift in team behavior over six months. Analysts began volunteering interpretations and questions in team settings. The quality of analytical output improved because people were surfacing uncertainty earlier in the process rather than hiding it. The 90th percentile engagement score included specific recognition of the psychological safety the team felt.
impact: Observable shift to high-trust team culture; 90th percentile engagement score; analytical output quality improved
fullStory: The moment I knew the psychological safety work was paying off was a team meeting where an analyst raised her hand and said, 'I'm not sure I understand why we're approaching this problem this way.' A year earlier, that question would never have been asked in a team setting — it would have been whispered to a colleague after the meeting. The fact that she asked it in front of everyone, and the fact that the room didn't flinch, was the indicator I was looking for. The shift didn't happen because I gave a speech about psychological safety. It happened because I asked a question I didn't know the answer to in a team meeting, and I was wrong about something in front of the whole group, and I said 'I was wrong, here's what I should have done differently.' That was slide one of the actual content. The policies and practices followed from that foundation.

title: Reframing Capital Markets Risk Through Independent Analysis
situation: As the inaugural Insights Analyst for Capital Markets at OMERS — reporting directly to the CFO rather than to the investment teams — my mandate was to provide independent oversight of the $105B pension fund's capital markets portfolio. The CIO was presenting a portfolio narrative built around effective cash yield, showing strong, stable income generation. This was the primary lens through which capital allocation decisions were being evaluated.
obstacle: The effective cash yield metric was obscuring significant structural risk. The portfolio carried large long CDS positions that created a substantial negative effective duration — meaning the portfolio was highly sensitive to interest rate movements in a direction opposite to what the income narrative implied. Combined with a levered beta above one from concentrated large-cap equity exposure, the true risk profile was materially more aggressive than the cash yield framing suggested. As a relatively junior analyst providing independent oversight, challenging the CIO's preferred narrative required both the analytical rigor to make the case and the political judgment to make it effectively.
action: Built an independent expected return framework grounded in total return analysis. Calculated the portfolio's weighted beta (which came out above one) and modified duration (which was negative due to the CDS positions). Identified the specific mechanism: large long CDS positions were effectively creating a short duration posture that would benefit from rising rates but would be hurt by falling rates — a risk that the cash yield metric made completely invisible. Prepared a clear analytical presentation of the alternative view and brought it directly to the CFO, framing it as a complementary total-return perspective alongside the income view.
result: Reframed internal evaluation of capital markets performance at the CFO level. Leadership developed a more complete understanding of the portfolio's true risk profile — specifically the hidden duration and leverage exposures that the income narrative had obscured.
impact: Capital markets risk framework fundamentally reframed; hidden duration risk from CDS positions and levered equity exposure surfaced to CFO
fullStory: The dynamic at OMERS was one of the most politically complex I've navigated. My role was explicitly to provide independent analytical oversight of the investment teams — which made me useful to the CFO and uncomfortable for the investment leadership, particularly the CIO. When I identified that the cash yield narrative was masking a significant negative duration position and levered beta, I had a clear choice: file it as an observation or bring it to the CFO. I brought it to the CFO. The framing mattered enormously: I wasn't saying the portfolio strategy was wrong — I was saying the measurement framework was incomplete. The effective cash yield told part of the story. Total return, duration, and beta told the rest. Presenting both perspectives and letting the CFO draw conclusions was the right approach — it preserved my credibility as an analytical resource rather than an political actor, while ensuring the information reached the person who needed it.
notes: Strong capital markets fluency story

title: Institutional-Grade Yield Forecasting Model — Demographic Drivers of Long-Term Returns
situation: In the Capital Markets analytics role at OMERS, I was responsible for reviewing and assessing investment strategy across multiple dimensions — strategy, macro, micro, and portfolio — for a $105B pension fund making long-horizon capital allocation decisions. A critical question was what interest rate and equity yield environments to expect over the 20-30 year horizon most relevant to pension ALM.
obstacle: Long-horizon yield forecasting is fundamentally different from short-horizon market prediction. Most available models were built for investment horizons of 1-3 years and relied heavily on cyclical factors — business cycles, monetary policy, short-term inflation expectations. For a pension fund concerned with meeting obligations 20+ years out, those short-cycle models were of limited relevance. The structural drivers of long-run yields — savings rates, productivity, demographic aging — were not adequately captured in standard analytical approaches.
action: Led research linking demographic factors to long-term interest rate and equity yield profiles. Built a proprietary model mapping age-cohort disbursement patterns to savings behavior and its macroeconomic effect on long-run yields. Integrated academic research on demographic drivers of the secular decline in real interest rates (particularly the work documenting the relationship between working-age population share and savings rate) with portfolio analysis specific to OMERS' liability structure. The model produced scenario-based long-run yield forecasts under different demographic trajectory assumptions.
result: Produced a durable long-horizon analytical framework for capital allocation and ALM decisions that went beyond what standard models offered. The model provided a demographic lens on long-term asset allocation that remained analytically useful years after it was built — and has remained in personal use as a fundamental analytical tool.
impact: Proprietary demographic yield forecasting model built; long-horizon ALM analytical capability strengthened
fullStory: The demographic yield forecasting work was one of the most intellectually satisfying projects of my career because it connected macroeconomics and behavioral economics to a genuinely practical problem: what return environment should a pension fund plan for over the next 30 years? The key insight — which had been documented academically but wasn't widely embedded in institutional investment practice — was that the secular decline in real interest rates that had been ongoing since the early 1980s was substantially driven by demographic factors: the baby boom moving through its peak savings years, combined with population aging in developed economies, systematically suppressed the natural rate of interest by creating excess savings relative to investment demand. If that mechanism is right, the demographic trajectory tells you something important about where long-run yields are heading. I built the model to formalize that relationship and apply it to OMERS' specific situation. It became one of the tools I used in every ALM conversation I've had since.
notes: Distinctive capital markets and long-horizon modelling story

title: Protecting Institutional Credibility — Correcting Misapplied GIPS Standards
situation: During my role as the independent Capital Markets analyst reporting to the CFO, I identified that a senior leader was misapplying GIPS (Global Investment Performance Standards) in the presentation of capital markets performance to the investment committee. The misapplication was not obvious — it required technical knowledge of GIPS standards to identify — but its effect was to present performance in a way that materially overstated results relative to the correct GIPS-compliant calculation.
obstacle: Challenging a senior leader's performance reporting methodology — when that methodology was benefiting their narrative — was politically complex. The leader had organizational authority significantly above mine. Raising it internally through normal channels risked being dismissed as an analytical technicality. Raising it externally to the CFO risked both political fallout and potential damage to the leader's relationship with me that could affect my effectiveness in the role.
action: Conducted a thorough technical review of the GIPS standards application and documented the specific deviation — the incorrect handling of composite construction and the resulting performance misstatement. Approached the leader privately first, presenting the technical findings clearly and giving them the opportunity to correct the presentation voluntarily. When the leader pushed back and maintained the original methodology, escalated respectfully to the CFO with the documented technical analysis. Made clear that the issue was a standards compliance matter with institutional reputation implications, not a personal critique.
result: The CFO directed correction of the performance reporting methodology. The GIPS-compliant presentation was adopted. The institutional credibility of OMERS' performance reporting to the investment committee was protected. The CFO acknowledged the importance of the finding.
