impact: GIPS standards compliance restored; institutional performance reporting integrity protected
fullStory: The GIPS situation required exactly the kind of courage that most analytical professionals avoid deploying because it's uncomfortable. I knew the answer to 'what happens if I raise this' was 'political friction.' But I also knew the answer to 'what happens if I don't raise this' was 'an institutional investor's investment committee is making decisions based on non-compliant performance data.' When you frame it that way, the answer is obvious. The approach mattered: I gave the leader the opportunity to fix it privately. That was genuine — I wasn't looking for a fight, I was looking for the right outcome. When they pushed back, I escalated — not because I wanted to escalate, but because the institution's credibility was the actual client I was protecting. The CFO's response validated the escalation.
notes: Strong ethical judgment and political navigation story

title: Navigating a Toxic Leadership Chain With Professional Integrity
situation: During part of my OMERS tenure, I worked in an environment where the leadership chain above me was actively working against me — excluding me from meetings where my analytical work was being discussed, misrepresenting my contributions to senior stakeholders, and in some cases using political capital to limit my visibility with the CFO. This was not incidental friction — it was a sustained pattern of sabotage by a leadership chain that perceived my independent analytical position as a threat to their influence.
obstacle: The sabotage was subtle enough to be deniable but consistent enough to be clearly intentional. Escalating directly risked looking like sour grapes without compelling evidence. Confronting the behavior directly risked making it worse. The CFO — my actual client — was not seeing the full picture because information was being filtered. The only sustainable path was to maintain output quality and political independence while the organizational dynamics worked themselves out.
action: Focused completely on the quality and impact of the analytical work — making it compelling enough that the CFO sought it out regardless of what the leadership chain was saying. Built alliances with leaders outside the toxic chain — people who interacted directly with the CFO and who could provide context when needed. Documented significant examples of the sabotage behavior without being reactive about it. Maintained professionalism consistently, not sinking to the tactics being used against me. The CFO eventually began interacting with me more directly, which progressively reduced the intermediary's ability to filter.
result: Reputation as an analytical resource to the CFO remained strong despite the sabotage effort. Multiple senior leaders commented on departure that I had navigated the environment with exceptional grace and professionalism. The relationships built outside the toxic chain outlasted the political situation.
impact: Professional integrity maintained throughout; CFO relationship preserved; reputation grew stronger than the opposing narrative
fullStory: I want to be honest about what navigating political sabotage actually requires: it requires choosing not to fight when fighting would make things worse, which is one of the hardest professional disciplines I know. Every instinct says to defend yourself, to expose what's happening, to go over someone's head. Sometimes the right answer is none of those. The analytical work was my protection. If my output quality was high enough and my relationship with the CFO was direct enough, the intermediary's ability to damage me was limited. So I focused obsessively on the work. I documented the behavior, but I didn't escalate every instance — I held it as context. The people I built relationships with outside the chain were the real safeguard: they provided the CFO with a picture of my work and character that the official line manager couldn't fully distort. On the way out, several of those leaders told me I'd handled the environment better than almost anyone they'd seen in a similar situation. I told them the truth: it was hard. But it was the right call.
notes: Strong resilience and political navigation story

title: P&L Advisory — Serving as De Facto CFO for Participant Outcomes
situation: The Participant Outcomes (formerly Member Retention) business was a P&L-accountable unit responsible for rollover products and participant engagement programs within Manulife Retirement. As AVP Advisory, I served as the primary financial advisor to the head of Participant Outcomes — functioning as their de facto CFO despite not holding that title formally. The business needed rigorous financial oversight for investment planning, operating plan management, and performance reporting to executive leadership.
obstacle: The Participant Outcomes leader was a strategist and commercial operator, not a financial professional. The business lacked a dedicated finance partner and had been relying on fragmented FP&A support from the broader finance team — which produced reporting that was technically correct but not strategically useful. Investment planning decisions were being made without a consistent financial framework. The CFO of Global Retirement — my direct boss — expected board-quality materials monthly.
action: Took ownership of all financial reporting and analysis for the Participant Outcomes P&L: monthly P&L reporting to the Global CFO and the business head, board-level materials summarizing performance against plan, operating plan development and assumption-setting for the annual planning cycle, and investment planning analysis to evaluate proposed programs against expected retention and revenue impact. Built financial models to evaluate the ROI of specific participant engagement initiatives — connecting the cost of outreach programs to their expected impact on rollover capture and AUM retention. Provided ongoing advisory on pricing, product strategy, and retention investment decisions.
result: The Participant Outcomes business developed consistent financial discipline and a clearer connection between its strategic investments and their expected returns. The CFO gained confidence in the business's financial management. Investment decisions were made with greater rigor — some programs that had been running on intuition were discontinued when financial analysis showed negative expected value.
impact: P&L advisory function established for Participant Outcomes business; investment decisions made with financial rigor; board-quality monthly reporting delivered
fullStory: The P&L advisory role was one of those situations where the title didn't match the responsibility. Officially I was an analyst-level advisor providing financial support. In practice I was doing the work of a business CFO: owning the monthly P&L, preparing board materials, challenging investment assumptions, and providing the financial framework within which the business head made decisions. I think of that role as the best preparation I had for more senior advisory work — because the Participant Outcomes leader was brilliant at the commercial side and needed a genuine financial thought partner, not just someone to generate reports. The conversations were substantive: why is the contribution margin on this product declining, what's the ROI case for doubling the outreach budget, how should we sequence investment given our capital constraints? Those are the conversations I wanted to be having, and the business head trusted me enough to have them honestly.

title: 25% Workforce Reduction — Preserving Value Through Design
situation: A business-wide mandate required a 25% headcount reduction across the Retirement analytics and insights function — approximately 5 positions from a team of 20. The explicit requirement from leadership was to maintain service and insight delivery quality while reducing cost. The implicit challenge was to execute a significant reduction while maintaining team morale and capability.
obstacle: A 25% headcount reduction that maintains output quality doesn't happen through simple headcount elimination — it requires fundamentally redesigning how work gets done. The risk was cutting people without cutting work, which leads to overloaded survivors and eventually worse outcomes than if the reduction hadn't happened. The emotional complexity was real: these were not poor performers being let go, they were good people in a smaller organization.
action: Before a single position was identified, mapped every process and workflow the team owned — reports, analyses, governance activities, meeting commitments. Categorized each by value-add and automation potential. Found significant portions of work that nobody would miss if they disappeared: reports that had been produced for years but were no longer used, processes that existed because 'we've always done it,' and manual steps in reporting workflows that were candidates for automation. Redesigned workflows, automated high-volume manual reporting, and eliminated low-value activities. Restructured roles around the remaining higher-value work. Identified where the reduction would fall based on role design rather than performance ranking — some roles simply became smaller or were combined.
result: 25% headcount reduction achieved. Output quality was maintained and in several areas improved — because the automation and process redesign that preceded the reduction had already made the function more efficient. The remaining team had clearer, more purposeful roles. Leadership acknowledged that the execution was handled with unusual discipline.
impact: 25% headcount reduction with maintained or improved output quality; workflow redesign freed capacity for insight work
fullStory: The principle behind this reduction was simple but not widely practiced: don't cut people and then figure out how to cover their work. Figure out how to cover their work first, and then the reduction becomes a natural consequence of that design. The workflow mapping exercise was revealing — we found that somewhere between 30-40% of what the team was doing every week was either automatable or simply not valuable enough to continue. That was uncomfortable to acknowledge, but it also meant the reduction wasn't about losing capability — it was about becoming more focused. The hardest part was the human element: having honest conversations with people whose roles were being changed or eliminated. I made those conversations direct and dignified. I told people the truth about what was happening, why, and what we were doing to support them. That's the standard you hold yourself to.
